revisions.from: that the resurrection that is going to happen will indeed be
revisions.to: that the future resurrection will take place
revisions.rationale: The Greek `τὴν μέλλουσαν ἀνάστασιν ἔσεσθαι` is a standard construction meaning 'the future resurrection will be/take place.' The draft's hyper-literalism creates a redundant and clunky English phrase ('going to happen will indeed be') that obscures the author's clear rhetorical prose. | Azure: The proposed rewrite is clearly closer to the Greek idiom. In Κατανοήσωμεν … πῶς ὁ δεσπότης ἐπιδείκνυται … ἡμῖν τὴν μέλλουσαν ἀνάστασιν ἔσεσθαι, the participle μέλλουσαν already marks the resurrection

revisions.from: You shall not desire the things of your neighbor
revisions.to: You shall not covet the things of your neighbor
revisions.rationale: In the context of echoing the Decalogue, ἐπιθυμέω (with 'the things of your neighbor') specifically denotes illicit, acquisitive desire. 'Covet' captures this negative nuance perfectly, whereas 'desire' is too neutral and loses the specific force of the commandment. | Azure: The proposed change is closer to the Greek and to what the Didache’s audience would hear. Οὐκ ἐπιθυμήσεις with τὰ τοῦ πλησίον clearly echoes the Decalogue’s prohibition against illicit desire for a ne

revisions.from: ill-disposed
revisions.to: malicious
revisions.rationale: The Greek `κακοήθης` carries a stronger sense of active malice, spite, or craftiness (BDAG: 'malicious, crafty') than the somewhat mild and passive English 'ill-disposed.' 'Malicious' better matches the severity and register of the surrounding vices in this catechetical list. | Azure: The proposed change is lexically better grounded in the Greek. κακοήθης denotes bad character in an active sense—'malicious,' 'crafty,' 'spiteful'—rather than merely having an unfavorable disposition.

revisions.from: make a schism
revisions.to: cause division
revisions.rationale: The Greek σχίσμα in the 1st/2nd century referred to factions or divisions within the community (cf. 1 Cor 1:10), not yet the formal institutional 'schism' the English loanword now implies. 'Cause division' better reflects the author's intended meaning for an early Christian audience without importing later ecclesiastical baggage. | Azure: The proposed change is more faithful to what the Didache's audience would have heard. σχίσμα in early Christian Greek denotes a split, tear, or division within the community (as in 1 Cor 1:10), and he

revisions.from: remember night and day the one who speaks to you the word of God, and you shall honor him
revisions.to: you shall remember night and day the one who speaks to you the word of God, and you shall honor him
revisions.rationale: The Greek uses the future indicative for both commands (μνησθήσῃ and τιμήσεις), a common LXX pattern for imperatives. The draft translates the first as an imperative ('remember') and the second as a future ('you shall honor'). Translating both as 'you shall' maintains the author's consistent grammatical structure, which continues through the following verses. | Azure: The proposed change is supported by the Greek morphology and the Didache’s catechetical register. Both μνησθήσῃ and τιμήσεις are future indicatives functioning imperativally in the Jewish-Greek/LXX st

revisions.from: as to a pattern of God
revisions.to: as to a representative of God
revisions.rationale: While 'τύπος' can mean 'pattern' (as in a model to be copied), when applied to a person standing in the place of God's authority, it carries the sense of 'type', 'figure', or 'representative' (BDAG s.v. τύπος 6, explicitly citing this verse). 'Pattern of God' is awkward in English and obscures the author's point that the master visibly represents God's authority to the slave. | Azure: The proposed change is better grounded in the Greek. In ὡς τύπῳ Θεοῦ, τύπος is not naturally 'pattern' in the sense of an impersonal model to imitate; with a personal referent (the masters) it denotes

revisions.from: nor even be willing to look upon them
revisions.to: nor even be willing to look upon these things
revisions.rationale: The Greek pronoun αὐτὰ is neuter plural, referring to the occult practices, omens, or implements themselves. It is not masculine plural, which would be required to refer to the practitioners (the enchanter, astrologer, etc.). In English, 'them' naturally points back to the people just listed, creating an ambiguity that the Greek avoids. Translating as 'these things' accurately reflects the neuter pronoun. | Azure: The rationale is grammatically correct. αὐτὰ is neuter plural accusative, so it naturally refers not to the masculine personal designations just listed (ἐπαοιδός, μαθηματικός, περικαθαίρων) as persons

revisions.from: after being filled
revisions.to: after you are satisfied
revisions.rationale: The aorist passive infinitive ἐμπλησθῆναι (from ἐμπίμπλημι) in the context of a meal specifically means to be satiated or satisfied with food (BDAG ἐμπίμπλημι 1.b.α). 'After being filled' is overly literal and risks a modern misunderstanding (e.g., spiritual filling), whereas the author is simply marking the physical completion of the meal before the concluding thanksgiving. | Azure: The proposed change is closer to the meal-context sense of the Greek. In Didache 10:1, Μετὰ δὲ τὸ ἐμπλησθῆναι introduces the post-meal thanksgiving, and ἐμπλησθῆναι (aorist passive infinitive of ἐμπίμ

revisions.from: a trumpet's voice
revisions.to: the sound of a trumpet
revisions.rationale: While φωνή often means 'voice,' when applied to an inanimate instrument like a σάλπιγξ (trumpet), it refers to its 'sound' or 'blast' (BDAG φωνή 1). Rendering it as 'voice' introduces an unnatural personification in English that the Greek does not carry. | Azure: The proposed change is more faithful to the Greek. In the phrase σημεῖον φωνῆς σάλπιγγος, φωνή has the ordinary sense of a sound/noise when connected with an instrument, not a personal 'voice'; BDAG i

revisions.from: an opening out
revisions.to: a spreading out
revisions.rationale: The noun ἐκπέτασις (from ἐκπετάννυμι) specifically means a 'spreading out' or 'stretching out' (like wings or hands). 'Opening out' obscures this lexical root and misleadingly suggests a rift or parting in the sky (which would typically be ἄνοιξις). 'Spreading out' preserves both the literal Greek and the early Christian association with the outstretched arms of the cross. | Azure: The proposed change is lexically better grounded. ἐκπέτασις, derived from ἐκπετάννυμι, denotes an extension, stretching out, or spreading out, not an "opening" in the sense of a rupture or parting. So

revisions.from: appoints a table in the Spirit shall not eat from it
revisions.to: orders a meal in the Spirit shall not eat of it
revisions.rationale: The Greek word τράπεζα is used here by metonymy for the food placed upon it (BDAG τράπεζα 2, 'meal, food'). The phrase ὁρίζων τράπεζαν refers to a prophet commanding that a meal be prepared. 'Appoints a table' is overly literal and obscures this meaning for the English reader, sounding like the designation of furniture rather than a demand for food. Adjusting 'from it' to 'of it' pairs better with 'meal'. | Azure: The proposed change is closer to the Greek sense. In Καὶ πᾶς προφήτης ὁρίζων τράπεζαν ἐν πνεύματι, οὐ φάγεται ἀπ᾽ αὐτῆς, τράπεζα can indeed mean not merely a physical table but a table set with food,

revisions.from: the ways of the Lord. So then, from their ways
revisions.to: the conduct of the Lord. So then, from their conduct
revisions.rationale: The Didache relies heavily on the term ὁδός ('Way') for its foundational moral framework (the Two Ways). Here, the author uses τρόπος (BDAG: way of life, conduct, behavior). Translating τρόπος as 'ways' blurs this crucial lexical distinction for the English reader. Rendering it as 'conduct' (or 'character') preserves the author's specific meaning and avoids confusion with the ontological 'Ways' of chapters 1-6. | Azure: The proposed change is lexically justified. The Greek here uses τρόπους / τρόπων, not ὁδός, and in this context τρόπος means manner of life, conduct, or behavior rather than 'way' in the Didache's tec

revisions.from: add to righteousness
revisions.to: increase righteousness
revisions.rationale: The Greek noun δικαιοσύνην is in the accusative case, functioning as the direct object of the infinitive προσθεῖναι (to add / increase). The English phrasing 'add to' treats it as an indirect object or prepositional phrase. Translating it as 'increase righteousness' or 'add righteousness' correctly preserves the transitive grammatical relationship. | Azure: The reviewer’s grammatical point is sound. In εἰς δὲ τὸ προσθεῖναι δικαιοσύνην καὶ γνῶσιν Κυρίου, the infinitive προσθεῖναι takes δικαιοσύνην and γνῶσιν as its direct objects, so the sense is not 'add

revisions.from: And every prophet speaking in the Spirit you shall not test, nor shall you judge
revisions.to: And you shall not test or judge any prophet speaking in the Spirit
revisions.rationale: The Greek construction `πᾶς` with a negated verb (`οὐ πειράσετε`) is a Semitic idiom meaning 'not any' or 'none'. Rendering it literally as 'every... not' in English creates a scope ambiguity, potentially implying partial negation ('you should not test *every* prophet, just some'), whereas the author's intent is an absolute prohibition against testing *any* prophet speaking in the Spirit. | Azure: The proposed rewrite better captures the force of the Greek: Καὶ πάντα προφήτην λαλοῦντα ἐν πνεύματι οὐ πειράσετε οὐδὲ διακρινεῖτε uses πάντα προφήτην with negated future indicatives in a prohibitory

revisions.from: do not do to another.
revisions.to: you also do not do to another.
revisions.rationale: The Greek includes the emphatic pronoun with an adjunctive 'καί' (καὶ σύ, "you also" or "you yourself"), which heightens the personal responsibility in the negative Golden Rule. The current draft drops this emphasis. | Azure: The proposed change captures a real element present in the Greek: καὶ σὺ ἄλλῳ μὴ ποίει includes both the explicit pronoun σύ and adjunctive καί, which naturally gives an emphatic/adversative force suc

revisions.from: Neither pray as the hypocrites
revisions.to: Nor pray like the hypocrites
revisions.rationale: Azure-modified: The rationale is right in substance that Μηδὲ should not be left as the awkward standalone English 'Neither' here. In this catechetical sequence Μηδὲ functions as a continuing negative coordinator, so 'Nor' or 'And do not' better reflects the Greek discourse flow. Also, with a noun phrase such as οἱ ὑποκριταί, English 'like the hypocrites' is the more natural equivalent of ὡς in this context. I prefer 'Nor pray like the hypocrites' rather than 'And do not pray like the hypocrites' because it pre

revisions.from: Jesus the Messiah
revisions.to: Jesus Christ
revisions.rationale: For the Didache's intended audience of gentile converts in the late 1st or early 2nd century, 'Χριστός' functioned primarily as a proper name or fixed title ('Christ') rather than an explicit reference to the Jewish 'Messiah'. Translating it as 'the Messiah' imports a theological emphasis that the original gentile audience would not have primarily heard. | Azure: The source reads διὰ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ, a standard early-Christian formula in which Χριστός for this late-1st/early-2nd century Gentile church context is best taken as the fixed title/name "Christ," not a

revisions.from: Now concerning the thanksgiving, give thanks in this way.
revisions.to: Now concerning the thanksgiving, give thanks in this way:
revisions.rationale: The Greek text ends with a high dot (·), which functions here to introduce the specific liturgical formulas that immediately follow in verses 2 and 3. Using a colon in English preserves this direct introductory force better than a terminal period. | Azure: The Greek reads Περὶ δὲ τῆς εὐχαριστίας, οὕτως εὐχαριστήσατε· and the raised dot here functions as Greek punctuation introducing the formulas that follow immediately in 9:2–3. Since οὕτως ('thus/in th

revisions.from: of Father and Son and Holy Spirit
revisions.to: of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit
revisions.rationale: The Greek omits the articles here (Πατρὸς καὶ Υἱοῦ καὶ ἁγίου Πνεύματος) compared to 7:1, which is a standard grammatical feature in prepositional phrases and genitive chains (Apollonius's Canon). The entities remain entirely definite in the author's mind. Omitting the articles in English creates an artificially telegraphic register that the Greek does not have. Adding the articles accurately reflects the definiteness intended by the author and maintains consistency with the formula in 7:1. | Azure: The proposed change is more faithful to the Greek. In εἰς ὄνομα Πατρὸς καὶ Υἱοῦ καὶ ἁγίου Πνεύματος, the anarthrous genitives after ὄνομα do not make the referents indefinite; in this kind of preposit

revisions.from: Having first proclaimed all these things,
revisions.to: Having first said all these things,
revisions.rationale: The participle προειπόντες (from προλέγω) means 'to say beforehand' or 'tell in advance', referring here to the recitation or instruction of the preceding 'Two Ways' material (chs 1-6). Translating it as 'proclaimed' elevates the register to heraldic preaching (which would typically be κηρύσσω), whereas the context here is practical catechetical instruction and recitation. | Azure: The Greek participle προειπόντες is from προλέγω, whose basic sense is "say beforehand / say previously / tell in advance," not "proclaim" in the more heraldic or public-preaching sense associated wit

revisions.from: silver
revisions.to: money
revisions.rationale: While ἀργύριον literally means 'silver', in the context of general possessions and charitable giving, it functions as the standard term for 'money' (BDAG s.v. ἀργύριον 2). Translating as 'money' better conveys the practical nature of the instruction to the original audience. | Azure: The proposed change is closer to the likely sense of ἀργυρίου here. Although ἀργύριον literally denotes 'silver,' in Koine it commonly functions metonymically for 'money' or 'cash' (as noted in BDAG),

revisions.from: you will know him
revisions.to: you will know
revisions.rationale: The verb γνώσεσθε (you will know) is used absolutely here, carrying the sense of 'you will find out' or 'you will discern [the truth].' Supplying 'him' as a direct object in English ('you will know him') introduces a connotation of personal acquaintance, whereas the author's focus is strictly on discerning the visitor's true character after testing. | Azure: The Greek reads ἔπειτα δὲ δοκιμάσαντες αὐτὸν γνώσεσθε, with αὐτόν governed by the participle δοκιμάσαντες ('after testing him'), while γνώσεσθε stands without an expressed object. So the current Engli

revisions.from: But perform your prayers and your acts of mercy and all your deeds in this way
revisions.to: But as for your prayers and your alms and all your deeds, do them in this way
revisions.rationale: The Greek topicalizes the objects by placing them before the verb (Τὰς δὲ εὐχὰς... οὕτω ποιήσατε). Preserving this fronting maintains the author's emphasis. Additionally, ἐλεημοσύνας refers specifically to charitable giving to the poor (alms), a concrete practice echoing Matthew 6, whereas 'acts of mercy' is overly broad. Using 'do' (ποιήσατε) pairs naturally with 'alms' (the standard idiom is ποιέω ἐλεημοσύνην) and resolves the slight awkwardness of 'perform your alms'. | Azure: The proposed rewrite is closer to the Greek. The clause begins with fronted accusatives, Τὰς δὲ εὐχὰς ὑμῶν καὶ τὰς ἐλεημοσύνας καὶ πάσας τὰς πράξεις, before the imperative ποιήσατε, so reproducing som

revisions.from: their harmful works
revisions.to: their works
revisions.rationale: The provided Latin source text reads 'operationes eorum' (their works/deeds). The draft includes 'harmful', which appears to translate a variant reading (such as 'opera nocua', found in some Vulgate manuscripts) that is not present in the authoritative source text provided for this verse.

revisions.from: renew many things in them
revisions.to: renew many things in it
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'in ea' is feminine singular ablative, referring back to the eagle ('aquila', f.) or the earth ('terra', f.). It does not refer to the three kingdoms ('regna', neuter plural, which would require 'in eis'). The English must reflect the singular 'in it'.

revisions.from: these are the ten tribes that were led away captive from their own land in the days of King Josiah
revisions.to: these are the ten tribes that were led away captive from their own land in the days of King Hoshea
revisions.rationale: The author of 4 Ezra, a knowledgeable Jewish writer, originally wrote 'Hoshea' (the king of the northern kingdom during the Assyrian exile), which is preserved in the Syriac, Ethiopic, and Arabic daughter versions. The Latin 'Iosiae' (Josiah) is a well-known scribal corruption in the Latin transmission, not the author's original text. Since the translation's goal is authorial intent, and the instructions specify triangulating with daughter versions where the Latin is corrupt, the main text must reflect the author's intended 'Hoshea'.

revisions.from: And it happened when the forty days were completed, the Most High spoke to me, saying, “The first things that you wrote, set them out publicly, and let the worthy and the unworthy read them.
revisions.to: And it happened when the forty days were completed, the Most High spoke to me, saying, “The first things that you wrote, set them out publicly, and
revisions.rationale: The phrase "let the worthy and the unworthy read them" translates "legant digni et indigni", which in the provided Latin source text belongs to the beginning of verse 46, not verse 45. The translation for verse 45 should end with "and" (et) to match the provided source boundaries, leaving the rest for verse 46.

revisions.from: from him
revisions.to: with him
revisions.rationale: The Latin text reads 'cum eo' (with him). The draft translates this as 'from him' (which would be 'ex eo' or 'ab eo'). While 'from him' makes logical sense for Adam's descendants and may reflect a reading in one of the daughter versions (like the Syriac), translating the Latin base text requires 'with him' unless an emendation is explicitly adopted and noted.

revisions.from: neither darkness nor evening nor morning, neither summer nor spring nor heat nor winter, neither frost nor cold, neither hail nor rain nor dew,
revisions.to: neither air nor darkness nor evening nor morning, neither summer nor spring nor heat nor winter, neither frost nor cold, neither hail,
revisions.rationale: The English draft shifts verse boundaries against the provided source text. It omits 'aerem' (air), which begins the Latin source for verse 41, and adds 'pluuiam' (rain) and 'rorem' (dew), which belong to the Latin source for verse 42. The rewrite aligns the English exactly with the Latin source text assigned to this specific verse.

revisions.from: from whom they are to receive their glorious reward.
revisions.to: from whom they, being glorified, are to receive their reward.
revisions.rationale: The Latin adjective 'gloriosi' is nominative plural, modifying the subject (the righteous souls), not the accusative singular object 'mercedem' (reward). The English draft incorrectly transfers the attribute to the reward. The text emphasizes the glorified state of the believers themselves.

revisions.from: nor upon death, nor judgment, nor destruction
revisions.to: nor upon their death, nor their judgment, nor their destruction
revisions.rationale: The Latin accusatives `mortem`, `iudicium`, and `perditionem` are parallel to `plasma` and are implicitly governed by the same genitive `eorum` (of those). The author's point is not that God will ignore death or judgment in general—judgment is a central theme of 4 Ezra—but that He will not concern Himself with the death, judgment, or destruction *of the wicked*. The English draft's omission of 'their' creates a sweeping universal statement that distorts the author's theology. Adding 'their' restores the intended scope.

revisions.from: whose service is in wind and fire,
revisions.to: whose service turns to wind and fire,
revisions.rationale: The draft completely omits the Latin verb 'conuertitur' ('is turned' / 'is changed'). The author is alluding to the cosmological motif (cf. Psalm 104:4) where angels or their service take the form of wind and fire. Restoring the verb is necessary for accuracy and also helps distinguish this clause (which refers back to the angels via the plural 'Quorum') from the subsequent clauses (which refer to God via the singular 'cuius').

revisions.from: so also are the deeds of the present age:
revisions.to: so also is the course of the present age:
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'actus' (4th declension nominative singular here) refers to the 'course', 'pattern', or 'operation' of the present age. Translating it as 'deeds' wrongly implies human moral actions, which breaks the author's analogy comparing the earth's structural production of clay and gold to God's creation and salvation of humanity.

revisions.from: I call to witness the favor
revisions.to: I bear witness to the favor
revisions.rationale: The Latin verb 'testor' with an inanimate accusative object ('gratiam') means 'to bear witness to' or 'to attest' (Lewis & Short II), not 'to call to witness' (which would imply invoking the favor as a witness, L&S I). God is testifying to the grace or favor of the coming people, not invoking it.

revisions.from: and Malachi, who is also called the messenger of the Lord.”
revisions.to: and Malachi, who is also called the angel of the Lord.”
revisions.rationale: In 2nd–3rd century Christian Latin (the original language of 5 Ezra), 'angelus' overwhelmingly means 'angel,' not a generic human 'messenger' (which would typically be 'nuntius'). The author is referencing a specific early Jewish and Christian tradition (derived from the LXX of Mal 1:1) that Malachi was an angel. Translating 'angelus' as 'messenger' to align with the Hebrew etymology of Malachi's name obscures the Latin author's actual word choice and the specific tradition they are invoking. The footnote confirms this was a defensive translation choice.

revisions.from: And the men who have been taken up, who have not tasted death from their birth, will be seen
revisions.to: And they will see the men who have been taken up, who have not tasted death from their birth
revisions.rationale: The Latin `uidebunt` is active, third-person plural ('they will see'), with the survivors from the previous verse as the implied subject and `homines qui recepti sunt` as the object. The draft incorrectly renders it as a passive ('will be seen'), shifting the grammatical subject and losing the active experience of the survivors.

revisions.from: From Abraham to Abraham
revisions.to: From Abraham to Isaac
revisions.rationale: The Latin reading 'ab Abraham usque ad Abraham' is a clear scribal corruption, as the following clause ('because from him Jacob and Esau were born') refers to Isaac. Per project guidelines for 4 Ezra, when the Latin is corrupt, we triangulate with the daughter versions (Syriac, Ethiopic, Arabic), which preserve the correct reading 'From Abraham to Isaac'.

revisions.from: and those who have stored up faith were sealed—
revisions.to: and before those who have stored up faith were sealed—
revisions.rationale: The Latin verb `consignarentur` (sealed) is in the subjunctive, continuing the sequence governed by `antequam` (before). By omitting 'before' in the final clause, the English turns it into an independent declarative statement, losing the temporal subordination that God sealed the faithful *before* the world was created. Repeating 'before' in English restores the author's grammatical and theological sequence.

revisions.from: for coals of fire will burn upon the head
revisions.to: for he will burn coals of fire upon the head
revisions.rationale: The Latin verb `conburet` is third-person singular future active, and `comburere` is transitive ('to burn up, consume'). The subject is implied (God/the Lord), and `carbones ignis` is the accusative direct object. The draft incorrectly treats 'coals' as the nominative subject of an intransitive plural verb.

revisions.from: that my son, when he came into his bridal chamber, died and disaster befell him,
revisions.to: , ‘My son, when he entered his bridal chamber, died, and disaster befell him,’
revisions.rationale: The Latin `quod filius meus` reflects a Greek `hoti` recitativum introducing direct speech (hence the first-person `meus`). The English draft translates `quod` as "that" but keeps "my son," which confusingly makes it sound like the angel's son. It should be punctuated as a direct quotation, matching the treatment of `quoniam nutrivi` in verse 47.

revisions.from: made use of the evil heart.
revisions.to: had the evil heart.
revisions.rationale: The Latin verb *utor* (here *utebantur*, taking the accusative *cor malignum* in Late Latin syntax) when used with a quality or condition means 'to possess' or 'to have' (e.g., *bona valetudine uti*, 'to have good health'). Translating it as 'made use of' implies deliberately utilizing a tool, which distorts the author's theological concept of the 'evil heart' (*yetzer hara*) as an inherited, burdensome condition (cf. 3:21). 'Had' accurately reflects the idiom and the author's intent.

revisions.from: how can your vessel grasp the way of the Most High, and, already worn out by the corrupt age, understand incorruption?” And when I heard these things, I fell on my face,
revisions.to: how can your vessel grasp the way of the Most High, and, already worn out by the corrupt age, understand incorruption?” And when I heard these things, I fell on my face, and I said to him, “It would have been better for us not to exist,”
revisions.rationale: The Latin source text for verse 11 concludes with 'Et dixi illi: melius erat nos non adesse,' which the English draft omits entirely from this verse (it appears to have been shifted to verse 12 in the draft). To accurately reflect the source text assigned to this verse, this clause must be translated here.

revisions.from: those who are above the heavens
revisions.to: he who is above the heavens
revisions.rationale: The Latin relative pronoun 'qui' can be singular or plural. While the first instance is plural ('qui... inhabitant', 'those who dwell'), the second ('qui super caelos') translates a singular in the underlying Greek (reflected as singular in the Syriac and Ethiopic daughter versions). It refers to God (the Most High). Translating it as plural ('those who') obscures the fundamental apocalyptic contrast Uriel is drawing between earthly humanity and the heavenly Creator.

revisions.from: its destruction
revisions.to: its harvest
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'destrictio' (from destringo, to strip or gather) here translates the Greek for 'harvest' (confirmed by the Syriac ḥṣādā), establishing the extended agricultural metaphor of sowing, reaping, and the threshing floor that continues through verse 32. Translating it as 'destruction' (likely confusing it with the variant 'destructio') breaks the author's intended metaphor.

revisions.from: I bear witness openly to my Savior.
revisions.to: I openly call my Savior to witness.
revisions.rationale: In Latin, `testor` with a personal accusative (`saluatorem meum`) idiomatically means 'to call [someone] to witness' or 'invoke as a witness' (Lewis & Short). The draft's 'bear witness to' misconstrues the idiom, turning a solemn prophetic invocation of the Savior as a witness to Ezra's charge into a generic statement about testifying about the Savior.

revisions.from: And those in the mountains will perish by famine and will eat their own flesh
revisions.to: Your cities will be shattered, and all yours in the field will fall by the sword. And those in the mountains will perish by famine and will eat flesh
revisions.rationale: The English draft omits the first half of the Latin source text assigned to this verse (`ciuitates tuae conterentur, et omnes tui in campo gladio cadent`). Although the draft placed this clause in 15:56, it must be translated here to align with the source text span for 15:57. Furthermore, the modifier `suas` ('their own') falls across the verse boundary in 15:58, so 15:57 should strictly end with 'flesh'.

revisions.from: and to a man's thigh and a camel's hock.
revisions.to: and to a man's thigh and a camel's hock. And there will be great fear and trembling upon the earth,
revisions.rationale: The entire second half of the Latin verse ('et erit timor et tremor multus super terram') has been omitted from the draft of verse 36. Looking at the context, it appears this clause was accidentally shifted to the beginning of the draft for verse 37. It must be restored here to accurately reflect the source text's versification and content.

revisions.from: And there will be great fear and trembling upon the earth, and those who see that wrath will shudder, and trembling will seize them. After this mighty storm-clouds will be stirred up
revisions.to: And those who see that wrath will shudder, and trembling will seize them. And after this, abundant storm-clouds will be stirred up
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'And there will be great fear and trembling upon the earth' translates 'et erit timor et tremor multus super terram', which belongs to the end of verse 36 in the Latin source and must be removed here. Additionally, 'et post haec' is missing its conjunction ('And after this'), and 'copiosi' (abundant/plentiful) is better rendered as 'abundant' rather than 'mighty' to distinguish it from 'validae' (mighty) in verse 40.

revisions.from: will be lifted up, full of wrath and star, to destroy
revisions.to: full of wrath, and a star will be lifted up to destroy
revisions.rationale: In the Latin 'plenae irae et sidus', 'sidus' (neuter nominative) cannot be governed by 'plenae' (which takes the genitive 'irae'). 'sidus' is a nominative subject alongside 'nubes'. The draft's 'full of wrath and star' mistranslates the grammar, treating the star as the contents of the clouds rather than a co-subject being lifted up.

revisions.from: As you do to my chosen ones, says the Lord, so God will do to you and will hand you over to evils. And your children will die of hunger, and you will fall by the broadsword.
revisions.to: The wages of a prostitute are in your bosom; therefore you will receive repayment.
revisions.rationale: The draft has suffered a severe versification misalignment. The English provided for 15:55 actually translates the Latin of 15:56 and the first half of 15:57 (`Sicut facies electis meis... et tu rumphea cades`). The actual Latin source for 15:55 is `merces fornicariae in sinus tuos, propterea redditionem percipies`, which means "The wages of a prostitute are in your bosom; therefore you will receive repayment." (Note: `fornicariae` is the genitive of `fornicaria`, "prostitute", not the abstract noun "prostitution").

revisions.from: pour out a heavy star upon the earth, even their star
revisions.to: pour out a heavy tempest upon the earth, and their tempest
revisions.rationale: While 'sidus' primarily means 'star', it has a well-attested secondary sense of 'storm' or 'tempest' (Lewis & Short, sidus II.B). The verb 'effundent' (pour out) and the adjective 'copiosum' (abundant/heavy), along with the cloud imagery in surrounding verses, strongly select the 'tempest' sense. Translating as 'star' creates a nonsensical image in English of pouring out a heavy star.

revisions.from: and the cloud that he raised up in wrath, and the star appointed to bring destruction toward east and west, will be thrown into confusion.
revisions.to: along with the cloud that he raised up in wrath; and the star appointed to bring destruction will be violently assailed by the east wind, the south wind, and the west wind.
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'nubem' is accusative, making it the object of the active verb 'recludent' ('they will shut it in, and the cloud...'), not the subject of the passive 'violabitur'. Additionally, the draft omits 'notum' (the south wind) entirely and translates 'ab' as 'toward'. With the passive 'violabitur', 'ab' indicates agency or source ('by' or 'from'). The phrase 'ab orientalem notum et occidentem' (using Late Latin 'ab' + accusative) means 'by the east wind, the south wind, and the west wind'.

revisions.from: if you had not killed my chosen ones at all times, exulting with the striking of your hands and speaking over their death when you were drunk?
revisions.to: says the Lord, "if you had not killed my chosen ones at all times, exulting by clapping your hands and speaking over their death
revisions.rationale: The draft misaligns with the source verse boundaries: it omits 'dicit dominus' (says the Lord) which begins verse 53, and incorrectly pulls in 'when you were drunk?' which translates 'cum inebriata es?' from the source text of verse 54. Additionally, 'percussione manuum' in the context of 'exultans' refers to the malicious 'clapping of hands' (cf. Vulgate Ezek 25:6), not the overly literal 'striking of your hands.'

revisions.from: They will gather against her
revisions.to: Babylon and destroy her. They will gather against her
revisions.rationale: The Latin source for verse 44 begins with 'Babylonem et exterent eam.' The draft omits this entirely from this verse (likely having translated it at the end of verse 43 due to the versification split of 'usque / Babylonem'). It must be included here to accurately reflect the provided source span.

revisions.from: from the day of their march
revisions.to: from a day's journey away
revisions.rationale: The Latin phrase `dies itineris` is a standard measure of distance ('a day's journey'). Governed by the preposition `a` (used here to express distance or separation, 'at a distance of'), `a die itineris` means 'from a day's journey away.' It describes how far the terrifying hissing carries, not when it started. The draft mistranslates this as a temporal phrase.

revisions.from: “And because you saw that the larger head disappeared, this means that one of them will die on his bed, but still with torments.
revisions.to: the sword will devour them. For the sword of the one will devour him who is with him,
revisions.rationale: The verse splitting script has misaligned the English text with the Latin source. The English provided for 12:27 translates the Latin of 12:26 ('Et quoniam uidisti caput maius...'). The Latin source for 12:27 is 'gladius eos comedet. Vnius enim gladius comedet qui cum eo,'. The English must be updated to translate the actual source text of this verse.

revisions.from: And when he has reproved them, then he will destroy them. But he will deliver the remnant of my people with mercy, those who have been preserved within my borders, and he will make them rejoice until the end comes, the day of judgment, of which I spoke to you from the beginning.”
revisions.to: he will deliver my people with mercy, those who have been preserved within my borders, and he will make them rejoice until the end comes, the day of judgment, of which I spoke to you from the beginning. This is the dream,
revisions.rationale: The draft suffers from a major versification offset. It includes the translation of 12:33 ('et erit cum arguerit eos, tunc corrumpet eos. Nam residuum' -> 'And when he has reproved them, then he will destroy them. But he will deliver the remnant of') which is not present in the source text for 12:34. It also omits the final clause of 12:34 ('Hoc est somnium' -> 'This is the dream'), which the draft incorrectly pushed to 12:35. The rewrite strictly aligns the English with the Latin source span for 12:34.

revisions.from: and show your servant the interpretation
revisions.to: the interpretation
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'and show your servant' duplicates the translation of 'et ostende seruo tuo mihi', which is already correctly placed at the end of verse 7. Verse 8 begins directly with the accusative objects 'interpretationem et distinctionem'.

revisions.from: But the two that remained, the sword will devour them. For the sword of the one will devour him who is with him,
revisions.to: but yet this one also will fall by the sword in the last days. And because you saw
revisions.rationale: The English draft is completely misaligned with the Latin source chunk. The current English translates an earlier portion of the text ('Nam duo qui superauerunt...'). The actual Latin source for this verse is 'sed tamen et hic gladio in nouissimis cadet. Et quoniam uidisti', which must be translated here to maintain alignment with the source text.

revisions.from: to show me the end of the times and the last things of the times.”
revisions.to: to show me the end of the times and the last things of the times.” And he said to me, “This is the interpretation of this vision that
revisions.rationale: The English draft omits the second half of the Latin source text assigned to this verse. While standard English versification often shifts this clause to verse 10 (as seen in the provided context draft for 12:10), the translation must match the provided source string for verse 9.

revisions.from: this is the Anointed One, whom the Most High has kept for the end, against them and against their impieties. He will rebuke them for their injustices and will set their contempt before them.
revisions.to: And it will be, when he has reproved them, then he will destroy them. For the remnant
revisions.rationale: The English draft for 12:33 actually translates the Latin of 12:32 ('hic est unctus...'). The Latin source for 12:33 is 'et erit cum arguerit eos, tunc corrumpet eos. Nam residuum', which means 'And it will be, when he has reproved them, then he will destroy them. For the remnant'. The verses have been misaligned in the draft.

revisions.from: Therefore I have shown you these things; for there is a reward with the Most High. For after three more days I will speak further with you and explain to you weighty and wondrous things.”
revisions.to: And I departed and went into the field, greatly glorifying and praising the Most High for the wonders that he did from time to time,
revisions.rationale: The draft text for 13:57 is a duplicate of 13:56, likely due to a mechanical splitting error. The Latin source for 13:57 is 'et profectus sum et transii in campum...', which translates to 'And I departed and went into the field...'. The suggested rewrite provides the correct translation for the verse's actual source text.

revisions.from: Yet it is easier for one in danger to come into these things than to pass through life like a cloud and not see what has happened in the end.”
revisions.to: Yet it is easier for one in danger to come into these things than to pass from the world like a cloud and not see what has happened in the end.” And he answered me and said:
revisions.rationale: The Latin "pertransire ... a saeculo" means "to pass away from the world" (i.e., to die), using the ablative of separation "a saeculo" (from the age/world). Translating this as "pass through life" distorts the author's contrast between those who are left alive and those who die before the end, making it sound like drifting aimlessly through life rather than departing from it. Additionally, the source text includes the narrative frame "Et respondit ad me et dixit:" at the end, which the draft omits (likely moving it to verse 21 for traditional English versification, but it must be translated here to match the provided source string).

revisions.from: he will set before them
revisions.to: he will reproach them to their faces with
revisions.rationale: The Latin verb 'inproperabit' (impropero) means 'to reproach, upbraid, or taunt', not merely 'to set before'. The draft translates 'coram eis' ('before them') but loses the accusatory, hostile force of the verb entirely.

revisions.from: And behold, a wind arose from the sea, to stir up all its waves.
revisions.to: And behold, a wind arose from the sea, to stir up all its waves. And I looked, and behold, that wind brought up out of the heart of the sea something like the figure of a man.
revisions.rationale: The Latin text suffers from a major, universally recognized lacuna here, omitting the introduction of the 'man from the sea.' This omission makes the Latin of verse 3 ('ipse homo', 'that man') nonsensical, as the man has not yet been introduced. Following the project guidelines to triangulate with the daughter versions when the Latin is corrupt, the missing clause must be restored from the Syriac and Ethiopic (e.g., Syriac: 'And I looked, and behold, that wind brought up out of the heart of the sea as it were the likeness of a man'). Without this, the central figure of the entire chapter 13 vision is missing from his own introduction.

revisions.from: and not meeting it;
revisions.to: and not attaining them.
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'occurrentes' here (likely reflecting a Greek word for 'attain' or 'reach', as supported by the Syriac and Ethiopic versions) means 'attaining' or 'reaching', not 'meeting'. Additionally, 'eis' is plural, referring back to 'quae' (the things stored up), so it should be rendered as 'them' rather than 'it'.

revisions.from: And as for your seeing him gathering to himself another peaceful multitude,
revisions.to: And as for your seeing him gathering to himself another peaceful multitude, these are the ten tribes that were taken captive from
revisions.rationale: The draft omits the second half of the Latin source text assigned to this verse ('Haec sunt decem tribus, quae captiuae factae sunt de'), having pushed it into the draft for verse 40. To align with the provided source text boundaries, this clause must be translated in verse 39.

revisions.from: But even those of your people who are left, who are found within my holy border—
revisions.to: But even those of your people who are left, who are found within my holy border, shall be saved.
revisions.rationale: The Latin text is defective here, lacking a main verb for the subject clause. The Syriac ('shall live') and other daughter versions (Ethiopic, Arabic 'shall be saved') preserve the original verb. By leaving the sentence hanging with a dash, the draft translates a Latin transmission error rather than the author's intended meaning.

revisions.from: For in them is the spring of understanding, the fountain of wisdom, and the river of knowledge.”
revisions.to: you may hand them over to the wise among your people. For in them is the spring of understanding, the fountain of wisdom, and the river of knowledge.”
revisions.rationale: The Latin source for verse 47 begins with 'tradas eos sapientibus de populo tuo;' (the verb dependent on 'ut' at the end of verse 46). The draft absorbed this clause into verse 46, leaving verse 47 missing its first half. It must be restored here to match the Latin verse boundaries.

revisions.from: ten parts of it have already passed
revisions.to: nine of its parts have already passed
revisions.rationale: The Latin reading 'X' (ten) is a well-known scribal error for 'IX' (nine). Translating 'ten' creates a logical contradiction ('ten parts... and half of the tenth part') and breaks the math of the author's eschatological timeline, as verse 12 states that two and a half parts remain (12 - 2.5 = 9.5). The Syriac, Ethiopic, Arabic, and Armenian versions all preserve the correct reading 'nine', which should be adopted here to restore the author's intended meaning.

revisions.from: will bring about things worse than these in turn. For the weaker the age becomes through old age, the more evils will multiply
revisions.to: —worse things than these will happen again. For the weaker the age becomes through old age, the more evils will multiply
revisions.rationale: The draft treats the future passive verb `facientur` as an active transitive verb ('will bring about') and makes the 'evils' from verse 15 its subject. In the Latin, `facientur` means 'will be done' or 'will happen,' and its subject is `deteriora` ('worse things'). The apocalyptic theology of 4 Ezra emphasizes that escalating woes are destined to occur (a divine/eschatological passive), not that current evils naturalistically cause future ones. Using a dash connects this smoothly to the topicalized relative clause in the draft's verse 15 ('For the evils that you have now seen happen—worse things than these will happen again').

revisions.from: Then I answered and said before you, Lord,
revisions.to: Then I answered and said before you, Lord, "Behold, I will go,
revisions.rationale: The Latin source for verse 18 explicitly includes the clause `Ecce enim ego abibo,` ("Behold, I will go"). The draft omits this clause from verse 18 entirely, having shifted it to the beginning of verse 19. To maintain accurate verse boundaries and reflect the source text of this verse, the clause must be restored here.

revisions.from: Therefore if you will rule your understanding and instruct your heart, you will be kept alive and after death you will obtain mercy.
revisions.to: Now you are here, and your brothers are farther inland. If, therefore, you will rule your understanding and instruct your heart,
revisions.rationale: The English draft has misaligned verse boundaries. It omits the first half of the Latin verse 33 ('nunc uos hic estis, et fratres uestri introrsum uestrum sunt', "Now you are here, and your brothers are farther inland") and incorrectly includes the first half of verse 34 ('uiui conseruati eritis...', "you will be kept alive..."). The translation must match the source text of verse 33, leaving the apodosis for verse 34.

revisions.from: And now I see that the joy of the age to come will benefit few, but torments many.
revisions.to: And now I see that the joy of the age to come will belong to few, but torments to many.
revisions.rationale: The English phrasing 'will benefit few, but torments many' inadvertently makes 'torments' read as an active verb with 'joy' as its subject (i.e., the joy torments the many). If read as a noun, the ellipsis forces it to share the verb 'benefit' (i.e., torments will benefit many). The Latin 'multis autem tormenta' contrasts with 'ad paucos pertinebit... iocunditatem'—meaning the joy pertains to the few, but torments to the many. Changing the verb to 'belong to' allows the ellipsis to work correctly in English ('will belong to few, but torments [will belong] to many').

revisions.from: For you have a store of works laid up with the Most High, but it will not be shown to you until the last times.
revisions.to: who have shown contempt, and do not count yourself among those who are tormented. For you have a store of works laid up with the Most High, but it will not be shown to you until the last times.
revisions.rationale: The first half of the Latin verse ('qui speruerunt, neque connumeres te cum his qui cruciantur') is missing from the English of verse 77. It appears the drafter merged this clause into the end of verse 76, but to maintain accurate verse boundaries for reading surfaces and references, the English must be aligned with the Latin verse division.

revisions.from: but silver is more abundant than gold, and bronze than silver, and iron than bronze, and lead than iron, and clay than lead.’
revisions.to: silver and bronze, and iron also and lead and clay; but silver is more abundant than gold, and bronze than silver, and iron than
revisions.rationale: The automated verse-splitter misaligned the English text with the Latin source spans. The Latin for 7:56 begins with the list of materials (`argentum et æramentum...`) and ends mid-sentence at `et ferrum super` ("and iron over/than"). The English draft incorrectly shifted the first half of 7:56 into 7:55, and pulled the first half of 7:57 into 7:56. The translation must be realigned to match the exact Latin span of 7:56.

revisions.from: And if it is one of those who have shown contempt and have not kept the way of the Most High, and of those who have despised his law, and of those who hate those who fear him,
revisions.to: to him who gave it, to adore the glory of the Most High first of all. And if indeed it is one of those who have shown contempt and have not kept the way of the Most High, and of those who have despised the law,
revisions.rationale: The mechanical verse-splitting script misaligned the Latin source text. The provided Latin string begins with the end of 7:78 ('ad eum qui dedit...') and cuts off before the end of 7:79. The English draft correctly translates the standard boundaries of 7:79, but it does not match the provided Latin string. To match the provided source exactly, the English must include the end of 7:78 and omit the end of 7:79 (which spilled into the 7:80 source chunk). I have also added 'indeed' for 'quidem'.

revisions.from: For it will last about
revisions.to: For it will have a duration like
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'sicut' means 'like' or 'as', not 'about' (approximately). In apocalyptic literature, a 'week of years' (a heptad) is a precise, determined theological time period. Translating 'sicut' as 'about' introduces a quantitative approximation that undermines the author's exact apocalyptic determinism. Additionally, 'Spatium ... habebit' is literally 'it will have a duration/space', which is more precise than 'it will last'.

revisions.from: If you have very few precious stones, you will set them aside according to their number for yourself, but lead and clay are abundant.
revisions.to: If you have very few precious stones, will you add lead and clay to them to make up their number?
revisions.rationale: The English draft translates a corrupt Latin reading ('ad numerum eorum compones eos tibi, plumbum autem et fictile abundat') which breaks the logic of the dialogue. Ezra's response in 7:53 ('Lord, how can that be?') makes no sense as a reply to a statement that lead and clay are abundant. As Stone and Violet note, the Syriac and Ethiopic versions preserve the original sense: a rhetorical question asking if one would add lead and clay to precious stones to increase their number. (Note: The English draft follows standard English versification, so this clause translates Latin found in the 7:53 source span).

revisions.from: just as waves are greater in number than a drop.”
revisions.to: just as a wave is greater than a drop.” And he answered me and said,
revisions.rationale: The draft omits the dialogue tag `et respondit ad me et dixit:` ("And he answered me and said,") which is present at the end of this verse in the Latin source. (It appears the draft may have shifted it to 9:17, but verse boundaries must be respected). Additionally, the verb `multiplicat` is singular, indicating that `fluctus` (4th declension) is singular nominative here; thus, "a wave is greater" is grammatically more accurate than "waves are greater in number".

revisions.from: when I was preparing for those who now are, before the age was made for them in which they would dwell, no one contradicted me then, for there was no one.
revisions.to: And then, when I was preparing for them—for those who now exist—before the age was made for them in which they would dwell, no one contradicted me.
revisions.rationale: The draft omits `et tunc` ("and then") and `eis` ("for them"), flattening the Latin `et tunc cum essem parans eis, his qui nunc` ("And then, when I was preparing for them, for those who now exist"). Furthermore, the draft adds "for there was no one" at the end. The provided Latin source simply reads `et nemo contradixit mihi` ("and no one contradicted me"). While the Syriac adds an explanatory "because no one existed," the Latin is not corrupt here and does not contain this clause. The translation should reflect the primary Latin text.

revisions.from: has a beginning through an ending, and the ending is made manifest,
revisions.to: has an evident beginning and a manifest end,
revisions.rationale: The draft translates a corrupted Latin text (`per consummationem`). The critical text (Bensly 1895) reads `initium patet et consummatio manifesta` ("the beginning is evident and the end is manifest"), which is supported by the Syriac and other daughter versions. The current English "a beginning through an ending" is nonsensical in this context. The author's point is that both the beginning and the end of worldly things are clearly seen, just as the times of the Most High will be.

revisions.from: who bears the place of a man.
revisions.to: who bears the form of a man.
revisions.rationale: Latin `locum` ('place') is a well-known textual corruption here, reflecting a misreading of the lost Greek *typon* ('form') as *topon* ('place'). The daughter versions (e.g., Syriac) confirm the author's intended meaning was 'form' or 'image' of a man. We must translate the author's intent, triangulating past the Latin error.

revisions.from: and concerning your inheritance, for which I mourn, and concerning Israel, for whom I am grieved, and concerning the seed of Jacob, for which I am troubled.
revisions.to: which causes me pain, and concerning your inheritance, for which I mourn, and concerning Israel, for whom I am grieved, and concerning the seed of Jacob,
revisions.rationale: The draft shifts clauses across verse boundaries. It omits 'quod mihi dolet' (which belongs at the start of v. 16, referring back to 'populo' in v. 15) and pulls in 'propter quod conturbor' (which belongs at the start of v. 17, referring to 'semine Iacob'). To align with the Latin source text for verse 16, the translation must reflect exactly the clauses present in this verse's source span.

revisions.from: among those who confess you
revisions.to: among those who exist
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'confitentibus' ("those who confess") is a widely recognized inner-Latin scribal corruption of 'consistentibus' ("those who exist"). The original reading is confirmed by the consensus of the daughter versions (Syriac, Ethiopic, Arabic, Armenian), which all read "those who exist" to parallel "those born" (genitis). The draft's addition of "you" further entrenches the corrupt reading. Per the project guidelines to triangulate with daughter versions where the Latin is corrupt, this should be emended to restore the author's intended parallelism.

revisions.from: because you have humbled yourself, as is fitting for you,
revisions.to: you, and have not judged yourself among the righteous, so that you might be greatly glorified.
revisions.rationale: The English draft suffers from a verse-alignment error. It translates 'Quoniam humiliasti te, sicut decet' (which the provided Latin text places at the end of 8:47). The actual Latin source for 8:49 is 'te, et non iudicasti te inter iustos ut plurimum glorificeris.', which must be translated here to match the source string.

revisions.from: your creation is preserved in your fire and water
revisions.to: your creation is preserved in fire and water
revisions.rationale: The Latin is 'in igni et aqua tua creatio'. The adjective 'tua' (nominative feminine singular) modifies 'creatio', not 'igni et aqua'. The draft incorrectly applies 'your' to the fire and water.

revisions.from: but spare your people and have mercy on your inheritance, for you have mercy on your own creation.”
revisions.to: let it not be upon us, but spare your people and have mercy on your inheritance, for you have mercy on your own creation.”
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'super nos' completes the negative particle from 8:44 ('Non, domine... super nos' = 'Not [so], Lord, upon us'). It is a plea ('do not let this be upon us'), not a divine title ('Lord over us'). The draft omits it from 8:45 entirely, having incorrectly attached it to the end of 8:44. (Note: the draft for 8:44 will need to be adjusted to remove 'over us').

revisions.from: For to you paradise has been opened, the tree of life has been planted, the age to come has been prepared, abundance has been made ready, the city has been built, rest has been appointed, goodness has been perfected,
revisions.to: wisdom has been perfected beforehand. The root has been sealed up from you, weakness has been extinguished from you, and death has been hidden; Hades has fled, and
revisions.rationale: There is a severe verse alignment error in the draft. The English text provided for 8:53 is actually the translation of 8:52 ('Vobis enim apertus est paradisus...'). The actual Latin source text for 8:53 ('ante perfecta sapientia. Radix signata est a uobis...') has been shifted to the English of 8:54 and 8:55. The suggested rewrite translates the correct Latin source for 8:53.

revisions.from: For while I live I will speak, and while I have understanding I will answer. Do not look upon the offenses of your people,
revisions.to: Do not look upon the offenses of your people, but upon those who have served you in truth.
revisions.rationale: The draft's versification is misaligned with the provided Latin source. The sentence 'For while I live I will speak...' translates 'Dum enim uiuo loquar...', which is located in the source text of verse 24. The second half of verse 25 ('sed qui tibi in ueritate serui-') was incorrectly pushed into the English of verse 26. The English for verse 25 should strictly reflect its corresponding Latin source, completing the split word 'serui-erunt' within this verse for English readability.

revisions.from: And you separated them from each other, for the seventh part where the water had been gathered could not hold them both.
revisions.to: the name of the second you called Leviathan. And you separated them from each other, for the seventh part where the water had been gathered could not hold them both.
revisions.rationale: The draft completely omits the first clause of the Latin source for this verse ('nomen secundi uocasti Leuiathan'). While the draft for verse 49 appears to have absorbed this text, it must be translated here to accurately reflect the source text's verse boundaries.

revisions.from: and when the age that is beginning to pass away is sealed over, then I will show these signs: books will be opened before the face of the firmament, and all will see together.
revisions.to: when the humiliation of Zion is completed, and when the age that is beginning to pass away is sealed, I will perform these signs: the books will be opened before the face of the firmament, and all will see together.
revisions.rationale: The clause 'quando suppleta fuerit humilitas Sion' ("when the humiliation of Zion is completed") is present in the Latin source for verse 20 but missing from the draft (it was erroneously appended to the end of verse 19). It must be restored to its proper verse. Additionally, 'faciam' means "I will do/perform," not "I will show" (which would be 'ostendam'), and adding the definite article to "the books" better reflects the apocalyptic idiom (cf. Dan 7:10).

revisions.from: so that you may not hasten away from the last times.’”
revisions.to: so that you may not be hasty concerning the last times.’”
revisions.rationale: The phrases `in prioribus temporibus` and `a novissimis temporibus` are parallel, referring to the beginning of creation and the eschaton. Translating `a novissimis` as 'away from' takes the preposition in a local sense of separation, which destroys the parallel and contradicts the context (Ezra is impatient *for* the end, not trying to escape it). Here, `a` means 'in respect to' or 'concerning' (L&S ab, II.C), matching the parallel `in`.

revisions.from: From him we all have come, and the people whom you have chosen.
revisions.to: From him we all have come, the people whom you have chosen.
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'quem elegisti populum' features the antecedent ('populum') incorporated into the relative clause, standing in apposition to 'nos omnes' ('we all'). By inserting 'and', the draft separates 'we all' from 'the chosen people', creating two distinct groups. This breaks the author's core argument in this passage, which identifies 'we all' as Israel (the chosen descendants of Adam) in contrast to the 'rest of the nations' (the unchosen descendants of Adam, v. 56).

revisions.from: show your servant the end of your signs, of which you showed me part in the past night.”
revisions.to: of yours, of which you showed me a part in the past night.” And he answered and said to me, “Stand upon your feet, and you will hear
revisions.rationale: The English draft is misaligned with the Latin source text chunks. It includes the translation of 'Vt demonstres seruo tuo finem signorum' (which belongs to the source of 6:11) and completely omits the second half of 6:12 ('Et respondit et dixit ad me : surge super pedes tuos et audies'). The rewrite strictly translates the provided source text for 6:12.

revisions.from: And I looked, and behold, something like a lion roused from the forest, roaring; and I heard how it sent out a human voice to the eagle and said,
revisions.to: roaring; and I heard how it sent out a human voice to the eagle and spoke, saying: “Hear, and I will speak to you; the Most High says to you:
revisions.rationale: The English draft is misaligned with the Latin verse boundaries provided in the source. It incorrectly includes the end of 11:36 ('And I looked...') and omits the end of 11:37 ('Hear, and I will speak...'). Furthermore, the draft flattens the Semitic calque `dixit dicens` (reflecting Hebrew `wayyomer lemor`) into a simple 'and said,' losing the translationese syntax characteristic of 4 Ezra. The rewrite aligns the English strictly to the provided Latin span and restores the Semitic idiom.

revisions.from: Then the third rose up and held rule as the former ones had, and it too disappeared.
revisions.to: will hold your length of time, not even half of it. Then the third rose up and held rule as the former ones had, and it disappeared, and
revisions.rationale: The verse-splitting script misaligned the English text with the Latin source chunks. The English draft omits the first half of the Latin verse ('tenebit tempus tuum, sed nec dimidium eius.') which was incorrectly placed in 11:17, and it includes 'too' (from 'ipsa' in 11:19). The English must be realigned to match the exact Latin source span for 11:18.

revisions.from: And while they were planning, behold, one of the resting heads awoke, the one in the middle, for it was larger than the two other heads.
revisions.to: remained were planning among themselves to reign as well; and while they were planning this, behold, one of the resting heads,
revisions.rationale: The English draft is misaligned with the Latin verse boundaries. It omits the first half of verse 28 ('superauerunt apud semetipsas cogitabant et ipsae regnare', meaning 'remained were planning among themselves to reign as well') and incorrectly pulls in the first half of verse 29 ('quod erat medium, euigilabat...', meaning 'awoke, the one in the middle...'). The English must be realigned to translate exactly the Latin span of verse 28.

revisions.from: As in an olive orchard three or four olives are left on each tree,
revisions.to: As in an olive orchard three or four olives are left,
revisions.rationale: The Latin text (`in oliveto`) specifies 'in an olive orchard' but does not say 'on each tree'. Adding this phrase softens the apocalyptic hyperbole of extreme scarcity (only three or four olives left in an entire orchard) and inserts information entirely absent from the source.

revisions.from: As a respectable and very beautiful woman hates a prostitute, so righteousness will hate iniquity when she adorns herself, and will accuse her to her face when he comes who defends the one who searches out every sin upon the earth.
revisions.to: As a respectable and very beautiful woman is indignant toward a prostitute, so righteousness will be indignant toward iniquity when she adorns herself, and will accuse her to her face when he comes who defends the one who searches out every sin upon the earth. Therefore do not be like her or her works.
revisions.rationale: The draft completely omits the final sentence of the Latin source text provided for this verse ('Propterea nolite similari eam nec operibus eius.'). This appears to be a versification boundary error (the context shows the draft pushed this sentence to 16:52), but it must be translated here to match the provided source span.

revisions.from: will ripen, and who will tread it? For there will be great desolation in the places.
revisions.to: will yield itself to the vintage, and who will gather it? For there will be great desolation in places.
revisions.rationale: The draft translates 'adligabit' as 'tread', but the Latin verb is *alligo* (*adligo*), meaning 'to bind' or 'tie up' (likely referring to binding vines or gathering clusters). 'Tread' would require *calcabit*. Additionally, 'tradet se ad vindemiam' is literally 'will yield itself to the vintage,' which carries a vivid personification that is lost in the flattened 'will ripen.' Finally, 'in the places' for *locis* is awkward English; 'in places' is more natural.

revisions.from: Behold, the Lord knows all the works of men and their devices and their thoughts and their hearts.
revisions.to: all the works of men and their devices and their thoughts and their hearts. He who said, “Let the earth be made,” and it was made;
revisions.rationale: The English draft is misaligned with the Latin source chunk provided for this verse. It includes 'Behold, the Lord knows' (which belongs to the end of the 16:54 source chunk: 'Ecce dominus cognoscit') and completely omits 'He who said, "Let the earth be made," and it was made' (which is explicitly present in the 16:55 source chunk: 'Qui dixit: fiat terra, et facta est :'). The translation must match the exact source text boundaries provided for the verse.

revisions.from: He shut up the sea in the midst of the waters, and by his word he hung the earth upon the waters.
revisions.to: He who shut up the sea in the midst of the waters, and hung the earth upon the water by his word;
revisions.rationale: First, note a tooling error: the automated verse-splitting script misaligned the Latin source text and the English draft. The English draft correctly translates standard verse 59 ('Qui conclusit mare... super aquam uerbo suo'), while the YAML source field captured the wrong Latin span. Regarding the translation itself: the draft flattens the hymnic relative pronoun 'Qui' ('He who' / 'who') into an independent clause ('He shut up'). This breaks the continuous doxological syntax of verses 56-62, which are all relative clauses modifying 'the Lord' (v. 54). Additionally, 'super aquam' is singular ('upon the water'), not plural ('upon the waters').

revisions.from: For many of those who inhabit the earth will die by famine, and the sword will destroy those who survive the famine.
revisions.to: And they will lose their lives upon the earth, and the sword will scatter those who survive the famine.
revisions.rationale: The draft is translating a different Latin variant (the standard Vulgate 'A fame enim plurimi qui inhabitant terram interient, et gladius perdet...'). The provided critical source text reads 'et aporiant vitam super terram' (and they will lack/lose life upon the earth) and 'gladius dispersit' (the sword scatters / will scatter). The English must reflect the provided source text.

revisions.from: for two or three hours
revisions.to: two or three hours beforehand
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'ante horas duas vel tres' means 'two or three hours before' (indicating the onset of labor pains prior to birth). The draft mistranslates 'ante' as duration ('for').

revisions.from: Woe to those who are bound fast by their sins and covered over by their iniquities, as a field is hemmed in by a forest and its seeds covered by thorns, so that no man can pass through; it is shut off and given over to be devoured by fire.
revisions.to: your sins, nor let your iniquities prevail over you. Woe to those who are bound fast by their sins and covered over by their iniquities, as a field is hemmed in by a forest and its seeds covered by thorns, through which no man passes; it is shut off and given over to be devoured by fire.
revisions.rationale: The Latin verse begins with 'peccata uestra, nec supereleuent iniquitates uestrae' (completing the sentence from 16:77). The current English draft omits this entirely from 16:78, likely because the chapter-level draft grouped it with 16:77. To match the Latin verse boundaries in this per-verse YAML, the completion of the sentence must be included here. Additionally, 'per quem non transit homo' is a simple relative clause with an indicative verb ('through which no man passes'), rather than a result clause ('so that no man can pass through').

revisions.from: For behold, in a very little while iniquity will be taken away from the earth, and righteousness will reign over us.
revisions.to: righteousness will reign over us. Let not the sinner say that he has not sinned,
revisions.rationale: The English draft and Latin source are misaligned due to differing versification schemes. The English draft for 16:53 translates Latin 16:52 and the first half of 16:53, while omitting the second half of 16:53. To match the provided Latin source span (`iusticia regnabit in nos. Non dicat peccator non se peccasse,`), the English must be adjusted to reflect only those clauses.

revisions.from: and its fields will grow over with thorns, and its roads and all its paths will sprout brambles
revisions.to: and its fields will waste away, and its roads and all its paths will sprout thorns
revisions.rationale: The Latin verb 'invetero' means 'to grow old', 'decay', or 'waste away'. The draft incorrectly translates it as 'grow over with thorns', and then translates 'spinas' (thorns) in the next clause as 'brambles' to compensate for the repetition.

revisions.from: Therefore go into the city to your husband.”
revisions.to: Therefore go into the city to your husband.” And she said to me,
revisions.rationale: The Latin source for this verse includes the clause 'Et dixit ad me:' ('And she said to me:'), which introduces the woman's response. The draft omits this clause from verse 17 (shifting it to verse 18). To maintain verse-level fidelity to the source text, it must be translated here.

revisions.from: And as for her telling you about the misfortune of her son, this is the interpretation:
revisions.to: This woman whom you saw, this is Zion, whom you now see
revisions.rationale: The English draft is misaligned with the Latin verse divisions. The current English translates the second half of Latin verse 42 (`Et quoniam enarrabat tibi de casu filii sui, haec absolutio est:`). The Latin for verse 43 is `Haec mulier quam uidisti haec est Sion, quam nunc conspicis`, which means "This woman whom you saw, this is Zion, whom you now see".

revisions.from: that the Most High will do for those who dwell
revisions.to: of what the Most High will do to those who dwell
revisions.rationale: The Latin relative pronoun 'quae' here reflects a Greek neuter plural ('the things which'), not a feminine plural referring back to 'visiones'. The draft incorrectly makes 'dream-visions' the object of 'do', resulting in the awkward English concept of 'doing a dream-vision'. Furthermore, the dative 'his qui habitant' in this apocalyptic context refers to what God will do *to* the inhabitants of the earth (the events and judgments of the last days), rather than *for* them.

revisions.from: now you no longer see the form of a woman, but a city appeared to you being built.
revisions.to: now you no longer see the form of a woman, but a city appeared to you being built. And as for her telling you about the misfortune of her son, this is the interpretation:
revisions.rationale: The entire second half of the Latin verse ('Et quoniam enarrabat tibi de casu filii sui, haec absolutio est:') was omitted from the English draft for this verse. Looking at the context, it appears the chapter-splitting script misaligned the text, pushing this clause into verse 43. It must be restored to verse 42 to match the source.

revisions.from: Ask the earth
revisions.to: But you are grieving over one son. For ask the earth
revisions.rationale: The Latin source for verse 9 begins with 'autem contristaris in uno filio' ("But you are grieving over one son") and 'Interroga enim terram' ("For ask the earth"). The draft omits the first clause entirely from this verse (it appears to have been shifted to 10:8 in the draft, where it was also altered into a question) and leaves 'enim' untranslated. To accurately reflect the provided Latin text, syntax, and verse boundaries, the indicative statement and the conjunction must be restored here.

revisions.from: And he said to me, “Stand up like a man, and I will instruct you.”
revisions.to: I will instruct you.” And I said, “Speak, my lord; only do not
revisions.rationale: The English draft is misaligned with the Latin verse boundaries. The Latin source for 10:33 is 'commonebo te; et dixi: Loquere, domine meus, tantum me noli'. The phrase 'And he said to me, "Stand up like a man"' translates 'et dixit ad me: Sta ut uir et', which is located at the end of the Latin text for 10:32. To accurately reflect the source span for 10:33, the English must be adjusted to match the exact Latin span.

revisions.from: that suddenly her face shone exceedingly, and her appearance flashed, her countenance becoming radiant,
revisions.to: that behold, her face suddenly shone exceedingly, and the appearance of her face became flashing, so that even [I was greatly afraid of her].
revisions.rationale: 1) The draft omits 'ecce' ('behold'), a key apocalyptic marker. 2) It double-translates 'species... vultus eius' as two separate subjects ('her appearance... her countenance'). 3) It omits the Latin 'ut etiam' ('so that even') to 'smooth' the text. Per project guidelines, where the Latin is corrupt (breaking off mid-sentence), we must triangulate with the daughter versions. The Syriac and Ethiopic preserve the author's intended clause ('so that I was greatly afraid of her' / 'afraid to approach her'), which must be restored rather than erased for readability.

revisions.from: Does Babylon act better than Zion?
revisions.to: anything to anyone about how this way should be abandoned. Does Babylon act better than Zion?
revisions.rationale: The draft completely omits the first half of the Latin verse (`nihil nemini quomodo debeat derelinqui via haec`), which was incorrectly absorbed into the draft for verse 30. To respect the versification of the source text, this clause must be restored to verse 31. (Verse 30 should correspondingly be adjusted to end at 'and have not made clear' / `et non significasti`).

revisions.from: and the turning of the scale will be found at the point where it inclines.
revisions.to: and it will be found which way the pointer inclines.
revisions.rationale: The draft translates `puncti` (genitive, "of the point/pointer") as a locative phrase ("at the point"). The phrase `momentum puncti` refers to the movement of the pointer or indicator on the balance, and `ubi declinet` functions as an indirect question ("which way it inclines"). The draft's "found at the point where it inclines" misconstrues the grammar and creates an awkward tautology.

revisions.from: Or what other nation has known you besides Israel? Or what tribes have believed your covenants as this Jacob has?
revisions.to: Or has any other nation known you besides Israel? Or what tribes have believed the covenants like these tribes of Jacob?
revisions.rationale: The draft translates 'alia gens' as 'what other nation', but the Latin lacks an interrogative here (it means 'another nation' or 'any other nation'). Furthermore, 'haec' in 'haec Iacob' is feminine (functioning as the nominative plural for *hae* in Late Latin, or referring back to *tribus*) and cannot modify the masculine *Iacob*. It means 'these [tribes] of Jacob', not 'this Jacob'. Finally, 'testamentis' is simply 'the covenants', without the possessive 'your'.

revisions.from: And I answered and said,
revisions.to: what is above the height of the heavens.” And I answered and said,
revisions.rationale: The Latin source text for verse 22 begins with `quae super altitudinem caelorum.` (completing the angel's speech from verse 21). The English draft omits this entirely from verse 22. To align with the provided source text segmentation, it must be translated here (and correspondingly removed from the draft of verse 21).

revisions.from: “Are you in greater haste than the Most High? For your haste is for your own sake
revisions.to: “You do not hasten more than the Most High; for you hasten for your own sake
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'non festinas tu' is a declarative statement ('You do not hasten'), not an interrogative. The subsequent sentence uses 'Nonne' to explicitly mark a question, highlighting that this first clause is a statement. The draft's question alters the angel's rhetorical point: he is stating a fact that Ezra's individual impatience does not exceed God's providential haste for the many. Additionally, restoring the active verb 'hasten' for 'festinas' is closer to the source than the draft's nominalization ('your haste is').

revisions.from: and from all the peoples that have multiplied you acquired for yourself one people, and to this people whom you desired you gave the law approved above all.
revisions.to: and from all the birds that have been created you named for yourself one dove, and from all the cattle that have been formed you provided for yourself one sheep,
revisions.rationale: The English draft is misaligned by one verse. The current English translates the Latin of verse 27 ('et ex omnibus multiplicatis populis...'). The source text for verse 26 is 'et ex omnibus creatis volatilibus...', which must be translated as 'and from all the birds that have been created you named for yourself one dove, and from all the cattle that have been formed you provided for yourself one sheep'.

revisions.from: And he answered me and said, 'Ask the woman who bears, and she will tell you.
revisions.to: And he answered me and said, 'Ask a woman who bears, and she will tell you. For you will say to her, "Why
revisions.rationale: The draft omits the final clause of the Latin verse (`Dices enim ei : quare`, "For you will say to her, 'Why"), which has been improperly shifted to verse 52 in the English draft. To maintain accurate verse alignment with the source text, this clause must be translated in verse 51. Additionally, "a woman" is more appropriate than "the woman" for the general relative clause `quae parit`.

revisions.from: And now, Lord, why have you handed over the one to the many, and prepared the one root above the others, yet scattered your only one among the many?
revisions.to: and from all the peoples that have multiplied you acquired for yourself one people, and to this people whom you desired you gave the law approved by all.
revisions.rationale: The draft translation is completely misaligned; it translates the Latin of verse 28 ('et nunc Domine, ut quid tradidisti unum plurimis...') instead of the provided source text for verse 27 ('et ex omnibus multiplicatis populis...'). The translation must reflect the source text of verse 27, which concludes the 'out of all X, you chose one Y' pattern.

revisions.from: And it happened after seven days that the thoughts of my heart were again very grievous to me.
revisions.to: And I fasted seven days, wailing and weeping, as Uriel the angel had commanded me.
