revisions.rationale: The draft text contains the translation for verse 21 due to a verse-alignment error in the draft. The Latin source for verse 20 is 'et ego ieiunavi diebus septem ululans et plorans, sicut mihi mandavit Urihel angelus', which must be translated according to its own text.

revisions.from: And he said to me, 'You cannot.' And I said, 'Why, lord? Why then was I born? Or why did not my mother's womb become my grave, so that I might not see Jacob's trouble
revisions.to: him more than he who made him?' And I said, 'No, lord, but I have spoken in grief, for my inward parts torment me every hour as I seek to grasp the path of the Most High and to search out part of his judgment.' And he said to me, 'You cannot.' And I said, 'Why, lord? Or why was I born? Or why did my mother's womb not become my grave, so that I might not see Jacob's trouble
revisions.rationale: The draft translation completely omits the first half of the provided Latin source text (likely due to a verse-alignment error in the draft pipeline). Additionally, in the translated portion, 'aut quid nascebar' is rendered 'Why then was I born?', losing the connective 'aut' (or). The rewrite translates the entire source text provided in the YAML and restores 'or'.

revisions.from: and from all the lands of the world you chose for yourself one pit, and from all the flowers of the world you chose for yourself one lily,
revisions.to: And I said, 'Sovereign Lord, from every forest of the earth and from all its trees you have chosen one vine,
revisions.rationale: The English draft contains an off-by-one verse alignment error, providing the translation for verse 24 instead of verse 23. The Latin source for 5:23 (`et dixi: Dominator Domine, ex omni silva terrae et ex omnium arborum eius elegisti vineam unam`) translates to "And I said: Sovereign Lord, from every forest of the earth and from all its trees you have chosen one vine."

revisions.from: And I said, 'Speak, my lord.' And he said to me, 'You have become greatly carried away in your mind over Israel. Have you loved him more than he who made him?'
revisions.to: was the angel to me who had come to me previously on the past night, and he said to me, 'Hear me, and I will instruct you; pay attention to me, and I will add
revisions.rationale: Severe versification misalignment. The English draft for this verse translates the Latin of 5:33-34a, completely ignoring the Latin source text provided for 5:32. Bensly's Latin edition breaks verses 31-34 mid-sentence; the English must be re-segmented to match the exact Latin span of verse 32 ('est angelus... adiciam'). The suggested rewrite provides the exact translation of the Latin chunk belonging to this verse.

revisions.from: And she herself will say to you, "Different are those born in the strength of youth,
revisions.to: And she herself will say to you, "Different are those born in the strength of youth, and different are those born toward the time of old age, when the womb is failing."
revisions.rationale: The draft suffers from a severe text-alignment error caused by the mechanical splitting of Bensly's line breaks. The English draft for 5:53 translates the first half of logical verse 53 ('Et dicet tibi et ipsa...'), but completely omits the second half ('et alii qui sub tempus senectutis deficiente matrice'), which the draft erroneously pushed into 5:54. The rewrite restores the complete logical verse 53. Additionally, 'sub tempus' is better rendered 'toward the time' rather than 'at the time', capturing the accusative of approaching time.

revisions.from: I have been permitted to tell you these signs; and if you pray again and weep as now, and fast for seven days, you will hear again things greater than these.
revisions.to: They will labor, but their ways will not prosper. I have been permitted to tell you these signs; and if you pray again and weep as you do now, and fast for seven days, you will hear again things greater than these.
revisions.rationale: The draft omits the opening clause of the source text, 'laborabunt et non dirigentur uiae eorum' (likely due to a versification boundary error, as it appears in the draft for v. 12). It must be restored here to match the source text. Additionally, the Latin idiom 'dirigentur uiae' reflects a Hebrew idiom for success or prosperity (e.g., Gen 24:40), so 'their ways will not prosper' captures the author's intent better than a literal 'be directed.'

revisions.from: And I said, 'No, lord, but I have spoken in grief, for my inward parts torment me every hour as I seek to grasp the way of the Most High and to search out part of his judgment.'
revisions.to: ...before you.' And I said, 'Speak, my lord.' And he said to me, 'You have become greatly distressed in mind over Israel. Or have you loved him more...
revisions.rationale: The English draft is completely misaligned, translating the Latin of verse 34 instead of verse 33. The source text for 5:33 is `coram te. Et dixi: loquere, dominus meus...` which must be translated here. The current English (`And I said, 'No, lord...`) translates `Et dixi: non, domine...` from verse 34.

revisions.from: and from all the birds that have been created you named for yourself one dove, and from all the cattle that have been formed you provided for yourself one sheep,
revisions.to: and from all the depths of the sea you filled for yourself one stream, and from all the cities that have been built you sanctified Zion for yourself,
revisions.rationale: The draft text is translating the Latin of verse 26 ('et ex omnibus creatis volatilibus...'). The verse alignment in the draft is off by one. The English must translate the Latin of verse 25 ('et ex omnibus abyssis maris...').

revisions.from: The nations will be jealous, but they will be able to do nothing against you, says the Lord.
revisions.to: Others will weep and be sorrowful, but you will be cheerful and abundant. The nations will be jealous, and they will be able to do nothing against you, says the Lord.
revisions.rationale: The English draft completely omits the first half of the Latin source text assigned to this verse (`alii plorabunt et tristes erunt, tu autem hilaris et copiosa eris.`). While the draft accidentally appended this translation to the end of verse 26, it must be restored here so the English aligns with the source text boundaries of verse 27.

revisions.from: Care for the broken and the weak; do not mock the lame; shelter the maimed, and admit the blind to the sight of my glory.
revisions.to: do not mock the lame; shelter the maimed, and admit the blind to the sight of my glory. Keep the old and the young within your walls.
revisions.rationale: The English draft is misaligned with the Latin verse boundaries provided in the source text. It includes 'Care for the broken and the weak' (translating *Confractum et debilem cura*, which is in 2:20) and omits *Senem et iuuenem intra muros tuos serua* (which the draft pushed to 2:22). To match the provided Latin for 2:21, the English must be adjusted. (Note: *claudum* is at the end of 2:20, so 'the lame' is supplied here as the object of *inridere noli*).

revisions.from: None of the servants whom I have given you will perish, for I will require them from your number.
revisions.to: your children; strengthen their feet. As for the servants whom I have given you,
revisions.rationale: The draft suffers from a severe verse-segmentation error. The English provided for 2:25 actually translates the end of 2:25 and the first half of 2:26 ('nemo ex eis interiet...'), while the first half of 2:25 ('filios tuos, confirma enim pedes eorum') was incorrectly appended to the English of 2:24. The rewrite aligns the English strictly with the Latin source span provided for 2:25 so that the mechanical alignment can be corrected.

revisions.from: And the Sodomitic sea will cast out fish, and by night it will give a voice that many have not known, but all will hear its voice.
revisions.to: And the Sea of Sodom will cast out fish, and by night it will give a voice that many do not know, but all will hear its voice.
revisions.rationale: The Latin adjective 'Sodomiticum' functions as a geographical descriptor. In modern English, 'Sodomitic' carries strong behavioral connotations that distract from the simple geographical reference to the city. 'Sea of Sodom' is the natural English phrasing for this geographical adjective and preserves the author's allusion without the modern baggage.

revisions.from: nor one who has grown old any longer
revisions.to: nor does one who has grown old bear any longer
revisions.rationale: The ellipsis in 'nor one who has grown old any longer' is slightly awkward. Supplying the verb 'bear' clarifies the clause and provides a better anchor for 'any longer' (translating 'adhuc').

revisions.from: And chaos will come
revisions.to: And a chasm will open
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'chaos' here reflects the Greek χάος or χάσμα (a gulf or chasm), which in turn translates a Hebrew word for a physical fissure in the earth. This is a standard apocalyptic physical sign, supported by the Syriac ('peḥtā', chasm). Translating it with the modern English abstract sense of 'chaos' (disorder) obscures the author's intended physical imagery of the earth opening up.

revisions.from: And that one will say no. And at that time men will hope and not obtain; they will labor, and their ways will not be directed.
revisions.to: passed through—righteousness, or one who does righteousness?' And it will say no. And it will be at that time that men will hope and not obtain;
revisions.rationale: The English draft suffers from a verse boundary misalignment. It omits the translation of 'pertransiit iustitia, iustum faciens ?' (which belongs at the start of 5:12, completing the question from 5:11) and incorrectly includes 'they will labor, and their ways will not be directed', which translates 'laborabunt et non dirigentur uiae eorum' from the beginning of 5:13. Additionally, 'iustum faciens' reflects a Semitic idiom for 'one who does righteousness' (Greek ποιῶν δικαιοσύνην), not 'making a man righteous' as the shifted 5:11 draft had it.

revisions.from: after the third upheaval
revisions.to: after the third trumpet
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'turbatam' ('disturbed' / 'upheaval') is a well-known scribal corruption of 'tubam' ('trumpet'). The daughter versions (Syriac, Ethiopic, Arabic) all read 'trumpet', reflecting the lost Greek σάλπιγξ and the author's original apocalyptic imagery. The translation should restore 'trumpet' to reflect the author's intent.

revisions.from: the stone will give its voice
revisions.to: the stone will utter its voice
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'dabit vocem suam' is a literal translationese calque of the Hebrew idiom נתן קול (natan qol), meaning to 'utter a voice' or 'cry out' (as seen in Habakkuk 2:11). In English, 'give its voice' is unnatural; 'utter its voice' or 'cry out' correctly conveys the author's intended apocalyptic personification without sounding like a mechanical gloss.

revisions.from: Do not be anxious, for when the day of distress and anguish comes, others will weep and be sorrowful, but you will be cheerful and abundant.
revisions.to: Do not be anxious, for when the day of distress and anguish comes, others will weep and be sorrowful, but you will be cheerful and have abundance.
revisions.rationale: The Latin adjective 'copiosa' means 'well-supplied' or 'rich.' Applying 'abundant' directly to a person in English ('you will be... abundant') is awkward and obscures the meaning. Rendering it as 'have abundance' or 'richly supplied' better captures the intended contrast with the distress of others.

revisions.from: that your people may be sanctified, who were called from the beginning.
revisions.to: that your people, who were called from the beginning, may be sanctified.
revisions.rationale: In English, placing the relative clause 'who were called from the beginning' after the verb 'sanctified' creates a disjointed sentence. Moving it immediately after its antecedent ('your people') reflects the Latin syntax ('populus tuus, qui...') more naturally in English.

revisions.from: having rose and lily
revisions.to: having roses and lilies
revisions.rationale: Latin frequently uses the singular for plants and flowers in a collective sense ('rosam et lilium'). In English, countable flowers require the plural to convey this collective abundance naturally.

revisions.from: Give harmony and peace to us and to all who dwell on the earth, just as you gave them to our fathers when they called upon you in holiness, in faith and truth, becoming obedient to your almighty and all-virtuous name, and to our rulers and leaders upon the earth.
revisions.to: Give concord and peace to us and to all who dwell on the earth—just as you gave them to our fathers when they called upon you in holiness, in faith and truth—as we become obedient to your almighty and all-virtuous name, and to our rulers and leaders upon the earth.
revisions.rationale: The accusative plural participle ὑπηκόους γινομένους refers to the petitioners ('us'), not the genitive αὐτῶν ('our fathers'). The draft's syntax accidentally makes the ancient fathers the ones becoming obedient to 'our [current] rulers and leaders upon the earth,' which is grammatically incorrect and logically impossible. Em dashes clarify the parenthetical nature of the reference to the fathers. Additionally, 'concord' is the preferred translation for ὁμόνοια in 1 Clement, as it serves as the direct civic antonym to στάσις (rebellion/faction).

revisions.from: When the ears of grain are sown whose number is beyond counting, how great a threshing floor they will make!”
revisions.to: When ears of grain without number are sown, how great a threshing floor they will make!”
revisions.rationale: The relative clause 'whose number is beyond counting' is awkwardly separated from its antecedent 'ears of grain' by the verb. 'Ears of grain without number' is more natural English and directly reflects 'spicae quarum non est numerus'.

revisions.from: he struck, and his hands healed.
revisions.to: he struck, and his hands healed him.
revisions.rationale: The Greek verb ἰάομαι (healed) is transitive. In English, the phrase "his hands healed" can easily be misread intransitively (as if the hands themselves recovered from an injury). Supplying the implied object "him" (referring to the disciplined man in v. 6) restores the transitive force of the author's statement. (Alternatively, translating these gnomic aorists as present tense—"he strikes, and his hands heal"—would also resolve the ambiguity and match the present tenses in verse 6).

revisions.from: your seed will be many
revisions.to: your offspring will be many
revisions.rationale: In English, 'seed' functions as a mass noun and pairs awkwardly with 'many' (a count modifier). Translating `σπέρμα` as 'offspring' (or rendering the phrase 'your seed will be numerous') resolves the grammatical friction while accurately reflecting the author's meaning of descendants.

revisions.from: from the hand of the sword
revisions.to: from the power of the sword
revisions.rationale: The Greek phrase ἐκ χειρὸς σιδήρου is a Hebraism (quoting Job 5:20). While σίδηρος (iron) is often used by metonymy for 'sword,' and χείρ means 'hand,' the literal English combination 'hand of the sword' creates an awkward mixed metaphor. Translating χείρ as 'power' (a standard sense in such Hebraic idioms) yields 'power of the sword.' Alternatively, preserving the exact Greek lexical choices would yield 'hand of iron.'

revisions.from: confess concerning his transgressions
revisions.to: confess his transgressions
revisions.rationale: While περὶ literally means 'concerning', the English verb 'confess' naturally takes a direct object for the sins being admitted. 'Confess concerning' is awkward; 'confess his transgressions' accurately and naturally conveys the Greek idiom (ἐξομολογεῖσθαι περὶ τῶν παραπτωμάτων).

revisions.from: Your heart has gone too far
revisions.to: Your heart has utterly failed
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'excedens excessit' is a calque of a Hebrew infinitive absolute construction (via Greek participle + finite verb) expressing intensity. Furthermore, 'cor excedit' reflects the Semitic idiom for the heart/mind failing or being bewildered (translating Greek ἐξίστημι, cf. Gen 42:28). The angel's argument is from lesser to greater: Ezra's mind is completely bewildered by the present age, so he cannot possibly comprehend the Most High. 'Gone too far' misses both the intensive grammar and the idiom, wrongly implying a rebuke for overstepping rather than a statement about his cognitive limits.

revisions.from: or whether more has passed than is to come.
revisions.to: or whether more has passed over us.
revisions.rationale: The Latin reads `aut plura pertransierunt super [nos]` (with `nos` at the start of v. 46), meaning 'or whether more has passed over us.' This reflects a Semitic idiom for the passage of time (e.g., עבר עלינו). The draft paraphrases this into a balanced English clause ('than is to come') but loses the author's actual phrasing and the underlying idiom.

revisions.from: your concern was
revisions.to: you had a struggle
revisions.rationale: The original Greek text begins with 'ἀγὼν' (ἀγὼν ἦν ὑμῖν, "you had a struggle"), which appears to have been dropped by the OCR in the provided source text. The draft's "your concern was" is far too weak for 'ἀγὼν', which denotes an intense struggle, contest, or agony (cf. Col 2:1). The English should reflect the intensity of the Corinthians' past striving, which the author is contrasting with their present discord.

revisions.from: desire rather to fall into mistreatment themselves than to bring it upon their neighbors
revisions.to: desire that they themselves fall into mistreatment rather than their neighbors
revisions.rationale: The Greek `ἢ τοὺς πλησίον` parallels `ἑαυτοὺς` as the accusative subject of the implied infinitive `περιπίπτειν`. It means they prefer that they themselves fall into mistreatment rather than that their neighbors do. The draft's 'bring it upon' introduces an active agency against the neighbors that is absent from the Greek.

revisions.from: bear condemnation of themselves than of the harmony
revisions.to: bring condemnation upon themselves than upon the harmony
revisions.rationale: The idiom `κατάγνωσιν φέρειν` with the genitive means to bring or pronounce condemnation against someone, not to 'bear' (endure) it. The current phrasing misconstrues the action and results in awkward English.

revisions.from: who are the faith and the hope
revisions.to: and the faith and the hope
revisions.rationale: The Greek `ἥ τε πίστις` consists of the feminine nominative article `ἡ` plus the enclitic `τε` ("and the faith"), not a relative pronoun. The author is invoking "the faith and the hope of the elect" as additional living witnesses in the oath formula ("As God lives... and the faith and hope... live"), a striking rhetorical feature that the English obscures by turning it into a relative clause.

revisions.from: Do not count every sin of your male slaves and female slaves against them
revisions.to: Do not count any sin of your servants and maidservants
revisions.rationale: In LXX-influenced Greek, the negation `μὴ` paired with `πᾶσαν` is a Semitic idiom meaning "not any" or "no" (translating Hebrew לֹא ... כָּל). Rendering it as "not every" implies God should count *some* sins, which contradicts the petition. Furthermore, `δούλων σου καὶ παιδισκῶν` reflects standard LXX liturgical register (e.g., Joel 2:29) where "servants and maidservants" (or "male and female servants") is more appropriate than the clunky "male slaves and female slaves against them."

revisions.from: Therefore mourn with all your might. And now, since we all mourn and you are sorrowful, because we are all grieved, why are you grieved over one son?
revisions.to: She mourns most bitterly. And now, since we all mourn and are sad, since we are all grieved—but you are grieving over one son.
revisions.rationale: 1) The draft translates `lugete` as a plural imperative, which makes no sense addressed to a single woman whom Ezra is rebuking for mourning. It is a recognized corruption of `luget` ("she mourns"), continuing the description of Zion. The draft also adds "Therefore" without basis. 2) `tristes estis` (plural "you") is similarly out of place and represents a corruption of `tristes sumus` ("we are sad"), parallel to the surrounding first-person plural verbs. 3) The draft turns the contrastive statement `tu autem contristaris in uno filio` into a question ("why are you grieved...?"), adding "why" without basis in the Latin.

revisions.from: spare those who act impiously, but destroy your people and preserve your enemies
revisions.to: have spared those who act impiously, but have destroyed your people and preserved your enemies
revisions.rationale: The Latin deliberately shifts from the present tense ('sustines', "you endure") to the perfect tense for the subsequent verbs ('pepercisti', 'perdidisti', 'conservasti'). By flattening these into the present tense, the English loses the historical finality of God's actions. Specifically, the destruction of Jerusalem is a completed historical event ('perdidisti', "you have destroyed") that grounds Ezra's entire apocalyptic complaint, not a general ongoing habit.

revisions.from: And it happened, when you were leading his seed out of Egypt and bringing them to Mount Sinai,
revisions.to: And it happened, when you led his seed out of Egypt, you brought them to Mount Sinai.
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'adduxisti' is a perfect indicative ('you brought'), serving as the main verb of the sentence following the temporal 'cum' clause. The 'et' before it is apodotic, a common Semitism reflecting a Hebrew waw-consecutive (e.g., 'when you led... then you brought'). The draft incorrectly flattens 'adduxisti' into a dependent participle ('and bringing'), which leaves verse 17 as a sentence fragment and forces an unnatural run-on into verse 18.

revisions.from: came up over my heart
revisions.to: came up upon my heart
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'super cor meum' is a literal calque of the Hebrew idiom על לב (al lev, 'upon the heart' / 'to mind'). In English, thoughts coming 'over' the heart sounds as if they are bypassing or covering it. 'Upon my heart' (or 'into my heart') better preserves the Semitic idiom of thoughts arising within the mind/inner self.

revisions.from: you bent down the heavens and set the earth in place, and shook the world,
revisions.to: you bent down the heavens, shook the earth, and moved the world,
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'statuisti' (you established/set in place) interrupts the sequence of cosmic upheaval at Sinai. It reflects a known textual corruption in the lost Greek transmission, where ἔσεισας (you shook) was misread as ἔστησας (you established). The Syriac and Ethiopic versions preserve the correct reading ('shook'). The author intended 'shook the earth'. Consequently, 'commouisti' is better rendered 'moved' to avoid repetition.

revisions.from: fearful words
revisions.to: anxious words
revisions.rationale: While 'timorata' can be translated literally as 'fearful,' in modern English 'fearful words' is ambiguous and often means 'frightening words.' Ezra is speaking words full of trepidation or anxiety as he dares to question God. 'Anxious words' (or 'words of trepidation') conveys the author's intent without the modern ambiguity.

revisions.from: offer to you offerings
revisions.to: offer oblations to you
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'offer to you offerings' is somewhat clunky. While it reflects the Latin cognate 'offerre ... oblationes' (likely translating a Hebrew cognate construction), 'offer oblations to you' or 'make offerings to you' flows more naturally in English without losing the semantic weight.

revisions.from: walked in its own will
revisions.to: walked according to its own will
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'in uoluntate sua' is a literal rendering of a Semitic idiom (likely reflecting Hebrew 'walked in the stubbornness of their heart' or similar). 'Walked according to its own will' or 'followed its own will' is more natural in English while preserving the underlying idiom.

revisions.from: and my heart overflowed
revisions.to: and my heart failed me
revisions.rationale: In the Latin of 4 Ezra (reflecting Greek ἐξέστη / Hebrew root תמה or שמם), 'excessit cor' means the heart 'failed,' 'fainted,' or 'was dismayed'—describing psychological distress, shock, or loss of senses, not an 'overflowing' of emotion.

revisions.from: seen them abounding
revisions.to: seen them prospering
revisions.rationale: In English, 'abounding' used absolutely without an object is awkward. The Latin 'abundantes' here refers to the nations' material wealth and success, contrasting with Israel's fruitless labor. 'Prospering' or 'living in abundance' conveys this naturally.

revisions.from: the measure of what has passed exceeded
revisions.to: the measure that has passed has been far greater
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'quae transivit mensura' is literally 'the measure that has passed.' Translating 'superabundavit' as the intransitive 'exceeded' is awkward in English without an object, as it typically implies exceeding a limit or quota. In parallel with 'amplius' (greater), the verb indicates that the past time vastly outweighs the time remaining. 'Has been far greater' captures this comparative abundance naturally and preserves the perfect tense.

revisions.from: the said measure
revisions.to: the foretold measure
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'praedicta' means 'foretold', 'predicted', or 'aforementioned'. Translating it as 'the said measure' introduces a legalistic, bureaucratic register ('the said property') that is out of place in an apocalyptic oracle. 'The foretold measure' or 'the appointed measure' better fits the theological context of God's predetermined timeline.

revisions.from: Shall we grow idle in doing good
revisions.to: Shall we cease from doing good
revisions.rationale: The verb ἀργέω with the preposition ἀπό means 'to cease from' or 'leave off' (BDAG). 'Grow idle in doing good' implies continuing to do good but lazily, whereas the Greek means stopping the good works entirely.

revisions.from: he enclosed it
revisions.to: he enclosed the sea
revisions.rationale: The pronoun 'it' is grammatically ambiguous following the compound object 'the sea and the living creatures in it.' Specifying 'the sea' clarifies that the enclosing action (ἐνέκλεισεν, echoing God setting boundaries for the sea in Job 38:8) applies to the waters.

revisions.from: the most excellent and truly great being in understanding
revisions.to: the most excellent and exceedingly great work of his intelligence
revisions.rationale: The draft attributes `κατὰ διάνοιαν` ('in understanding') to man. However, the context of chapter 33 repeatedly emphasizes God's attributes in creation (33:3: `συνέσει`, `βουλήματος`, `διατάξει`, `δυνάμει`). Here, `διάνοια` refers to God's intelligence or purpose. The neuter substantives `τὸ ἐξοχώτατον καὶ παμμέγεθες` refer to man as the greatest 'thing' or 'work' produced by God's intelligence. Additionally, `παμμέγεθες` is better rendered 'exceedingly great' to capture the `παμ-` prefix and match the parallel in 33:3.

revisions.from: showed what is the prize of endurance:
revisions.to: pointed out the prize of endurance,
revisions.rationale: The Greek `ὑπομονῆς βραβεῖον ἔδειξεν` simply means 'showed' or 'pointed out the prize of endurance.' The addition of 'what is' unnecessarily expands the text and shifts the meaning toward explaining the nature of the prize rather than demonstrating or pointing to it. Additionally, the colon at the end is grammatically awkward since verse 6 contains its own main verb (`ἔλαβεν`) rather than just being a list of items fulfilling verse 5.

revisions.from: unwillingly
revisions.to: unintentionally
revisions.rationale: In the context of sin (ἡμάρτετε), ἄκων refers to involuntary or unintentional sins (reflecting the LXX cultic category of sinning ἀκουσίως, e.g., Lev 4:2). 'Unwillingly' misleadingly suggests sinning under compulsion or against one's active desire, rather than sins of inadvertence or ignorance.

revisions.from: and feed you, after killing you, to idols.
revisions.to: and feed them with meat sacrificed to idols.
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'cibabunt idolis occisam' translates the Greek concept of εἰδωλόθυτα (meat sacrificed to idols). The context is the historical Christian crisis of being forced to eat pagan sacrifices during persecution, not believers being killed and fed to idols. 'Occisam' (slain) refers to the sacrificed animal (implied carnem), not the believers.

revisions.from: to practice them no more forever
revisions.to: never to practice them again
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'obliuiscimini... iam agere eas sempiterno' uses a complementary infinitive ('forget... to do them anymore forever'). The draft's literal rendering is clunky in English; 'never to practice them again' or 'never to commit them again' captures the negative force of 'forget to do' naturally while preserving the temporal scope of 'iam... sempiterno'.

revisions.from: For Zion our mother, the mother of us all, is grieving in grief and has been brought very low in humiliation.
revisions.to: see our mourning and what has happened to us? For Zion, the mother of us all, is grieving bitterly and in humiliation
revisions.rationale: Versification misalignment: the English draft pushed the first half of the Latin source (`uides luctum...`) into 10:6 and pulled the verb `humiliata est` from 10:8 into 10:7. The rewrite restores the exact bounds of the 10:7 source text. Additionally, 'grieving bitterly' better captures the force of the Hebrew infinitive absolute calque (`in tristitia contristatur`) than the overly literal 'grieving in grief'.

revisions.from: But on the night that is coming tomorrow you shall remain here,
revisions.to: But tomorrow night you shall remain here,
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'Nocte... quae in crastinum futura est' translates literally to 'the night which is to be tomorrow.' The draft's 'the night that is coming tomorrow' is slightly redundant and clunky; 'tomorrow night' captures the exact temporal meaning more naturally.

revisions.from: made me come into this abundance of this ecstasy of mind
revisions.to: brought me into this great bewilderment of mind
revisions.rationale: Latin 'excessus mentis' (reflecting Greek ἔκστασις) denotes a displacement of the mind, bewilderment, or trance. In modern English, 'ecstasy' strongly implies joy, which contradicts Ezra's terror here. Additionally, 'multitudinem' indicates degree ('greatness'), and 'fecit uenire' is a causative ('brought me'). The current literal rendering is awkward and misleading.

revisions.from: turn back the wandering of your people
revisions.to: turn back those of your people who wander
revisions.rationale: The Greek `τοὺς πλανωμένους` is a masculine plural accusative participle referring to people ('the wandering ones'). In English, 'the wandering' is naturally read as an abstract noun (the act of wandering). Phrasing it as 'those... who wander' or 'the wanderers' clarifies that it refers to people.

revisions.from: gathered together in concord to the same place
revisions.to: gathered in one place in concord
revisions.rationale: ἐπὶ τὸ αὐτὸ is a standard idiom for 'together' or 'in one place'. Rendering it as 'to the same place' while also using 'gathered together' creates a redundancy that is slightly awkward in English.

revisions.from: eager for doing good
revisions.to: eager to do good
revisions.rationale: "Eager to do good" is the more natural English idiom for `προθύμους... εἰς ἀγαθοποιΐαν`, conveying the purpose/direction of `εἰς` with an action noun more smoothly than "eager for doing good."

revisions.from: it has not arisen upon the heart of man
revisions.to: it has not entered the heart of man
revisions.rationale: The phrase ἀνέβη ἐπὶ καρδίαν is a Hebraic idiom (reflecting עָלָה עַל־לֵב) meaning 'to come to mind' or 'to enter the heart' (BDAG s.v. ἀναβαίνω 1.b.β). 'Arisen upon the heart' is an overly literal calque that obscures the natural force of the idiom in English.

revisions.from: survived from the four beasts which I made
revisions.to: remains of the four beasts which I had made
revisions.rationale: The English 'survived from' is awkward and could be misread as surviving a threat posed by the beasts, rather than being the last surviving member of their group. The Latin 'superasti de' here carries the partitive sense of 'remains of' or 'is left of' (L&S II.B). Additionally, 'feceram' is pluperfect ('had made'), which better reflects the sequence of past events than the simple past 'made'.

revisions.from: And those who consent to them will be had in derision and reproach and trampled underfoot.
revisions.to: some of you, and they will feed you with things sacrificed to idols. And those who consent to them will be held in derision and reproach, and trampled underfoot.
revisions.rationale: The draft omits the first half of the verse (`quosdam ex uobis et cibabunt idolis occisam`), having incorrectly merged it into the draft for 16:69. Furthermore, `idolis occisam` (reflecting Greek εἰδωλόθυτα, with an implied 'carnem' or as a corruption of neuter plural 'occisa') means "things sacrificed to idols" (idol meat), not "killing you to feed to idols" as the 16:69 draft rendered it. This is a major historical and lexical point in 6 Ezra, referring to the Roman persecution test of eating idol meat. The missing text must be restored to 16:70 and correctly translated.

revisions.from: brother-murder
revisions.to: the murder of a brother
revisions.rationale: While 'brother-murder' is a literal calque of the compound ἀδελφοκτονίαν, 'the murder of a brother' is more natural English. It successfully preserves the author's rhetorical repetition of 'brother' (ἀδελφοί ... ἀδελφοκτονίαν) which would be obscured by the standard Latinate term 'fratricide'.

revisions.from: but upon Cain and upon his sacrifices he did not pay attention.
revisions.to: but to Cain and to his sacrifices he paid no heed.
revisions.rationale: In English, one does not 'pay attention upon' something. The verb προσέχω (to pay attention, give heed, regard) with ἐπί is better rendered with 'to' in English. Using 'paid no heed' or 'gave no regard' also better matches the formal, scriptural register of this LXX quotation than the colloquial 'did not pay attention'.

revisions.from: are not in small glory
revisions.to: are in no small glory
revisions.rationale: The Greek litotes 'οὐκ ἐν μικρᾷ δόξῃ' is more naturally and idiomatically rendered in English as 'in no small glory' rather than 'not in small glory.'

revisions.from: lest he seize like a lion
revisions.to: lest he seize you like a lion
revisions.rationale: The verb ἁρπάζω (seize, snatch away) is transitive. While the Greek leaves the object implicit (understood from the addressees, 'you who forget God'), English requires an object for 'seize' to avoid awkwardness and clarify the action.

revisions.from: What then, therefore,
revisions.to: What then
revisions.rationale: The Greek particles `οὖν ἄρα` work together to indicate a logical conclusion or inference (e.g., 'so then', 'what then'). Translating both individually as 'then, therefore' is redundant and disrupts the author's formal rhetorical register.

revisions.from: for those who patiently endure for him?
revisions.to: for those who patiently endure?
revisions.rationale: The Greek text reads `τοῖς ὑπομένουσιν` (an absolute participle, 'those who patiently endure' or 'those who wait'). The draft inserts 'for him', which does not exist in the Greek of this verse (though `αὐτόν` does appear in the following verse, 35:4).

revisions.from: patiently endure him
revisions.to: wait for him
revisions.rationale: When taking a personal accusative object (αὐτόν, referring to God), ὑπομένω carries the sense of 'wait for' or 'await' (a common LXX idiom for hoping in the Lord, e.g., Ps 36:9), not 'endure'. Translating it as 'endure him' awkwardly implies God is an affliction to be suffered, which distorts the author's meaning.

revisions.from: everyone who is left from all these things
revisions.to: everyone who remains out of all these things
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'left from' is a slightly awkward calque of the Latin 'derelictus... ex'. Rendering it as 'remains out of' or 'survives' provides more natural English while accurately conveying the sense of those who endure the apocalyptic woes.

revisions.from: desert from him
revisions.to: defect from him
revisions.rationale: The English phrase 'desert from' is awkward. The Greek verb αὐτομολέω carries the political/military sense of deserting or defecting; 'defect from him' naturally accommodates the preposition ἀπό (from) while preserving the exact nuance of the word.

revisions.from: Where shall I arrive
revisions.to: Where shall I go
revisions.rationale: While ἀφικνέομαι strictly means 'arrive' or 'reach', in this context (asking where one might flee to escape God's presence) 'Where shall I arrive' is unnaturally clunky in English. 'Where shall I go' naturally captures the intended meaning of reaching a destination.

revisions.from: that he showed him
revisions.to: that he had shown him
revisions.rationale: The sequence of pronouns in English ('he offered him... that he showed him') creates slight ambiguity. Translating the aorist ἔδειξεν in this relative clause as a pluperfect ('had shown') relative to the main verb (προσήνεγκεν) helps clarify the timeline and the actors (God having previously shown the mountain to Abraham) without needing to artificially insert 'God' as the subject.

revisions.from: the reasonings we carry on
revisions.to: the reasonings we form
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'carry on' is slightly colloquial and awkward in English when applied to 'reasonings' (διαλογισμῶν). Using 'form' or 'make' better captures the middle voice of ποιούμεθα (producing thoughts or deliberations within oneself) while maintaining the formal register of the letter.

revisions.from: did not become high-minded
revisions.to: did not become arrogant
revisions.rationale: The Greek verb μεγαλοφρονέω means 'to be arrogant, haughty, or proud' (BDAG). In modern English, 'high-minded' has shifted to mean 'having strong moral principles,' which inadvertently turns a negative trait Moses avoided into a positive trait he supposedly lacked. 'Arrogant' or 'proud' accurately conveys the intended contrast with his humility.

revisions.from: what humility has strength before God
revisions.to: what strength humility has before God
revisions.rationale: The Greek interrogative 'τί' functions here as an accusative of degree or inner object with the verb 'ἰσχύει' (meaning "how much it avails" or "what strength it has"). The current English phrasing reads as an interrogative adjective modifying "humility" (i.e., "which humility has strength"), which distorts the Greek syntax and the author's intended meaning.

revisions.from: kicked back
revisions.to: kicked
revisions.rationale: The verb ἀπολακτίζω means to kick out (like an unmanageable, overfed animal) or to rebel, quoting the LXX of Deut 32:15. In modern English, 'kicked back' is a strong idiom for 'relaxed', which completely obscures the author's metaphor of stubborn rebellion.

revisions.from: became broad and fat
revisions.to: grew broad and fat
revisions.rationale: In English, animals or people 'grow' fat rather than 'become' fat; 'grew' better captures the progressive, organic change of state implied by the aorist passives ἐπλατύνθη καὶ ἐπαχύνθη in this agricultural metaphor.

revisions.from: For because the speech concerns them, they will tremble and be shaken, for they know that their end must be changed.”
revisions.to: Because the speech concerns them, they will tremble and be shaken, for they know that their end must be changed.”
revisions.rationale: The Latin simply reads 'Quoniam' (Because/Since). The draft's 'For because' is redundant and awkward in English.

revisions.from: And on the second day again you created
revisions.to: And again, on the second day, you created
revisions.rationale: Placing 'again' immediately before 'you created' creates a slight ambiguity in English, making it sound as though God created the firmament a second time. Moving 'again' to the beginning of the clause clarifies that it functions as a discourse marker continuing the sequence of creation days.

revisions.from: exits of the age
revisions.to: portals of the world
revisions.rationale: In this list of physical creation, Latin 'saeculum' (reflecting Greek αἰών / Hebrew עוֹלָם) carries its spatial sense ('world' or 'universe'), not its temporal sense ('age'). 'Exitus' refers to the cosmological outlets or gates at the ends of the earth through which winds and stars pass (a common feature in Jewish apocalyptic cosmology, e.g., 1 Enoch 33–36). 'Exits of the age' creates a mixed metaphor that obscures the author's spatial meaning.

revisions.from: All these things I have spoken before you, Lord, because you said that for our sake you created the firstborn world.
revisions.to: Now I have spoken all these things before you, Lord, because you said that for our sake you created this world.
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'primogenitum saeculum' ('firstborn world') is a recognized textual corruption, likely a displacement of 'primogenitum' from verse 58. The Syriac, Ethiopic, and Arabic versions preserve the original reading 'this world' (reflecting Greek τὸν αἰῶνα τοῦτον). Following the Latin here distorts the author's cosmology. Additionally, the draft omits 'autem' ('Now' or 'But'), which marks the crucial transition from Ezra's historical summary to his theological complaint.

revisions.from: powers of movement
revisions.to: powers of the earthquakes
revisions.rationale: In apocalyptic and biblical translation Latin, 'motus' frequently translates Greek σεισμός (earthquake). The author is listing concrete cosmic and creational forces (winds, thunders, lightnings, angels); 'earthquakes' fits this apocalyptic inventory, whereas 'movement' introduces an abstract philosophical concept foreign to the author's intent.

revisions.from: was not yet there from you
revisions.to: was not yet from you
revisions.rationale: The phrase `erat abs te` indicates origin or agency ('was from you' / 'was made by you'). Adding 'there' makes the English clunky and slightly obscures the point that the human voice had not yet been brought into existence by God.

revisions.from: will be terrified with those who dwell in it
revisions.to: will be terrified along with those who dwell in it
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'cum' here is associative, indicating that both the earth and its inhabitants share the terror. The English 'terrified with' risks being misread as instrumental (i.e., the earth is terrified *by* the inhabitants). Adding 'along' clarifies the associative syntax.

revisions.from: the brightness of the sun to be made, the light of the moon, and the arrangement of the stars
revisions.to: the brightness of the sun, the light of the moon, and the arrangement of the stars to be made
revisions.rationale: In Latin, the infinitive 'fieri' precedes and governs the entire list of accusatives ('solis splendorem, lunae lumen stellarum dispositionem'). In English, placing 'to be made' immediately after the first item leaves the subsequent items dangling syntactically. Moving the infinitive to the end of the list clarifies that all three were commanded to be made.

revisions.from: a voice full of sound
revisions.to: a full, resounding voice
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'uocem plenissimam sonus' (literally 'a voice most full of sound') is a somewhat wooden calque. In English, 'a voice full of sound' is awkward; 'a full, resounding voice' better captures the superlative force of 'plenissimam' and the natural register of an apocalyptic audition.

revisions.from: desire for tastes of many kinds
revisions.to: manifold delights of taste
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'concupiscentia' (desire) here reflects a literal translation of Greek ἐπιθυμία, which in turn likely translates the Hebrew חֶמְדָּה (delight, pleasantness, cf. Gen 2:9 'pleasant to the sight'). Translating it literally as 'desire' in English awkwardly implies a subjective feeling was created, whereas the author is describing the objective pleasantness or delightfulness of the created vegetation.

revisions.from: and like a lamb before the one shearing it is voiceless
revisions.to: and as a lamb before its shearer is voiceless
revisions.rationale: The English construction 'like a [noun] ... is [adjective]' is grammatically awkward; 'as' pairs correctly with the following 'so' (translating the correlative ὡς ... οὕτως). Additionally, 'its shearer' is a more natural and equally accurate rendering of the substantival participle τοῦ κείραντος than 'the one shearing it.'

The literal rendering of the present participle (οἱ θεωροῦντές) with an aorist main verb (ἐξεμυκτήρισάν) creates an awkward tense clash in English ('see me mocked me'). Aligning them to the past ('saw me mocked me') resolves this. Furthermore, the singular 'head' (κεφαλήν) following a plural subject is a Greek distributive singular; English idiom requires the plural 'heads'.

revisions.from: who serves well many
revisions.to: who serves many well
revisions.rationale: The English word order 'serves well many' is unnatural. The Greek 'εὖ δουλεύοντα πολλοῖς' (serving many well) is better rendered with the adverb following the object in English.

revisions.from:
This one bears our sins
and suffers pain on our behalf,
and we considered him to be in toil
and in plague and in affliction.
revisions.to:
This one bears our sins
and suffers pain on our behalf,
and we considered him to be in suffering
and under a blow and in affliction.
revisions.rationale: In the context of this LXX quotation (Isaiah 53:4), 'πόνος' translates Hebrew 'mak'ob' (pain/suffering), not 'toil' (labor). Likewise, 'πληγή' translates 'nagua'' (stricken/smitten) and refers to a physical 'blow' or 'stroke', not a 'plague' (which implies an infectious epidemic in modern English). Rendering it as 'under a blow' accurately reflects the Greek noun while remaining natural in English.

revisions.from: lifting up to him hands that are pure and undefiled
revisions.to: lifting pure and undefiled hands to him
revisions.rationale: The Greek uses direct attributive adjectives (ἁγνὰς καὶ ἀμιάντους χεῖρας). Translating this as a relative clause ('hands that are...') is slightly wordy; 'pure and undefiled hands' is tighter and matches the Greek syntax perfectly.

revisions.from: honorably and blamelessly assigned to them
revisions.to: blamelessly honored by them
revisions.rationale: The draft mistranslates 'τετιμημένης' (perfect passive participle of τιμάω, 'to honor') as 'assigned', and turns it into an adverb 'honorably'. The phrase 'αὐτοῖς τετιμημένης' uses a dative of agent with the perfect passive, meaning 'honored by them' (i.e., they held their ministry in honor and fulfilled it blamelessly).

revisions.from: Therefore, for this reason,
revisions.to: For this reason, then,
revisions.rationale: "Therefore, for this reason" is tautological in English. The Greek postpositive οὖν simply connects the thought ("then" or "therefore"), while διὰ ταύτην τὴν αἰτίαν provides the reason. "For this reason, then," or "For this reason, therefore," is more natural while preserving the exact rhetorical force of the Greek.

revisions.from: those entrusted in Messiah by God appointed the persons mentioned before to such a work?
revisions.to: those entrusted in Messiah by God with such a work appointed the aforementioned persons?
revisions.rationale: The aorist passive participle πιστευθέντες ('entrusted') takes the accusative ἔργον τοιοῦτο ('such a work') as its direct object (BDAG s.v. πιστεύω 2.a.β). The phrase means 'those who were entrusted with such a work.' The draft incorrectly attaches 'such a work' to the main verb κατέστησαν ('appointed'), adding the preposition 'to' which is absent in the Greek, and thereby shifting the 'work' from the apostles (who were entrusted with it) to the appointed presbyters.

revisions.from: bearing witness together with the ordinances laid down by him
revisions.to: bearing witness to the ordinances laid down by him
revisions.rationale: The dative phrase 'τοῖς ὑπ' αὐτοῦ νενομοθετημένοις' is the object of the participle 'συνεπιμαρτυροῦντες' (bearing witness to / attesting to), not a co-witness. The prophets are attesting to the laws Moses enacted, not witnessing alongside them (BDAG συνεπιμαρτυρέω).

revisions.from: Men, brothers
revisions.to: Brothers
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'Ἄνδρες ἀδελφοί' is a standard respectful address in Greek (frequent in Acts). Translating it literally as 'Men, brothers' is unnaturally clunky in English; 'Brothers' accurately conveys the author's intended register.

revisions.from: And when you were thirsty, did I not split the rock, and waters flowed in abundance?
revisions.to: When you were thirsty, did I not split the rock, so that waters flowed in abundance?
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'Nonne' introduces a question, but the English draft mixes an interrogative clause ('did I not split') with a declarative one ('and waters flowed') ending in a question mark. Rendering 'et' as 'so that' (or 'and did waters not flow') resolves the grammatical clash and captures the consecutive force of the action. Additionally, there is no conjunction ('And') before 'Nonne' in the Latin source.

revisions.from: Since the heavens declare the glory of God, and the firmament proclaims the work of his hands, day pours forth speech to day
revisions.to: The heavens declare the glory of God, and the firmament proclaims the work of his hands; day pours forth speech to day
revisions.rationale: The word `εἰ` at the beginning of the source text is an OCR error for the capitalized article `Οἱ` (a common misread of `Οἱ` in older printed Greek editions). By translating this spurious `εἰ` as "Since," the draft turns the first half of the Psalm quotation into a dependent clause, which either leaves the sentence as a fragment or falsely makes "day pours forth speech" the apodosis. The text is a direct quotation of Psalm 19:1-3 (LXX 18:2-4) and should simply begin "The heavens declare..."

revisions.from: commanded us not to lie will much more not himself lie
revisions.to: commanded not to lie will much more not lie himself
revisions.rationale: The Greek `ὁ παραγγείλας μὴ ψεύδεσθαι` does not contain an explicit object ('us'), and 'will much more not himself lie' is slightly awkward in English. Removing the supplied 'us' and adjusting the word order of 'himself' (translating the emphatic `αὐτὸς`) produces a more natural and strictly accurate rendering.

revisions.from: All we like sheep went astray;
revisions.to: We all went astray like sheep;
revisions.rationale: "All we" is an archaic English syntactic holdover from older Bible translations. "We all went astray like sheep" is the natural modern English equivalent of πάντες ὡς πρόβατα ἐπλανήθημεν, preserving the exact meaning without the archaic register.

revisions.from: Therefore speak to them and say,
revisions.to: But you, speak to them, saying: Thus says the Lord:
revisions.rationale: The draft translates 'Tu uero' as 'Therefore', but 'vero' is an adversative or emphatic transitional particle ('But you' or 'Now you'), not an illative ('Therefore' would be *ergo* or *igitur*). Furthermore, the draft completely omits the prophetic formula 'haec dicit dominus' ('thus says the Lord') from this verse, likely shifting it to verse 13. It must be retained here to accurately reflect the source text's syntax and versification.

revisions.from: “Do not ask anything further, Ezra.”
revisions.to: “Do not ask anything further, Ezra.” And I answered and said, “O sovereign Lord, if I have found favor
revisions.rationale: The draft omits the second half of the verse present in the Latin source ('Et respondi et dixi : O dominator domine, si inueni gratiam'), likely due to a verse boundary error that pushed it into the draft of verse 11. It must be restored here to match the source versification. Additionally, 'dominator domine' is the standard Latin rendering for the underlying Greek δέσποτα κύριε / Hebrew Adonai YHWH, best translated as 'O sovereign Lord'.

revisions.from: shaken
revisions.to: revolving
revisions.rationale: While σαλεύω often means 'shake' or 'agitate', in astronomical and cosmological contexts (as here, describing the orderly movement of the heavens), it refers to the regular motion or rolling of celestial bodies. Translating it as 'shaken' introduces a sense of violent disturbance that directly contradicts the author's argument about cosmic harmony and peace (ἐν εἰρήνῃ) in this chapter.

revisions.from: So then, let our souls be bound to this hope in the One who is faithful
revisions.to: In this hope, then, let our souls be bound to the One who is faithful
revisions.rationale: The verb προσδέω ('bind to') takes the dative for the object to which something is bound. Here, that object is `τῷ πιστῷ` ('to the faithful One'). The phrase `Ταύτῃ τῇ ἐλπίδι` is a dative of sphere or means ('In this hope'). The draft incorrectly treats 'this hope' as the object of the binding and translates `τῷ πιστῷ` as 'in the One,' distorting the Greek syntax.

revisions.from: nursed me
revisions.to: conceived me
revisions.rationale: The verb κισσάω originally refers to the cravings of a pregnant woman, and by extension in the LXX (which Clement is quoting here from Psalm 50:7) it translates the Hebrew יָחַם ('to conceive'). It means 'to conceive' or 'to be pregnant with', not 'to nurse' (which would be θηλάζω).

revisions.from: not to those who throw themselves upon his fear.
revisions.to: not to those who exalt themselves over his flock.
revisions.rationale: The provided Greek source contains severe OCR corruption ('ἐπιρριπμένων ἐπὶ τὸν φόβον' instead of 'ἐπαιρομένων ἐπὶ τὸ ποίμνιον'). The actual text of 1 Clement 16:1 is 'οὐκ ἐπαιρομένων ἐπὶ τὸ ποίμνιον αὐτοῦ' (not of those who exalt themselves over his flock). The translation must reflect the author's actual words, which directly address the Corinthian crisis of arrogant leaders exalting themselves over the church.

revisions.from: and look upon the heights in the air
revisions.to: and whose upper chambers are in the air
revisions.rationale: The draft inserts 'look upon', treating 'superiora' as the object of God's gaze. However, 'superiora' is nominative, parallel to 'oculi'. It translates Greek ὑπερῷα (reflecting Hebrew עליות, 'upper chambers'), referring to God's heavenly dwelling (cf. Ps 104:3, Amos 9:6). The text describes God's cosmic architecture, not his line of sight.

revisions.from: destroy the one who was formed with such labors by your command, by an easy decree
revisions.to: destroy by an easy decree the one who was formed with such great labors by your command
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'facili ordine' (by an easy decree) modifies 'perdideris' (you destroy), contrasting the effortless ease of destruction with the immense effort of creation. Placing it at the end of the English clause awkwardly separates it from 'destroy' and creates a dangling modifier. Additionally, 'tantis laboribus' is better rendered 'such great labors' to fully capture the magnitude of 'tantis'.

revisions.from: And now I will speak: concerning every man you know better, but concerning your people my pain is greater,
revisions.to: And now I will surely speak: concerning every man you know better, but concerning your people, for whom I am pained,
revisions.rationale: The draft breaks a four-fold rhetorical parallel. The author lists four titles for Israel, each followed by a relative clause of mourning (v. 15-16: populo... quod mihi dolet; hereditate... propter quam lugeo; Israel... propter quem tristis sum; semine... propter quod conturbor). Translating `quod mihi dolet` (pulled from v. 16 to complete the clause) as "my pain is greater" invents a comparative and severs the first item from the litany. Furthermore, `dicens dicam` reflects a Hebrew infinitive absolute ("I will surely speak") which the English flattens.

revisions.from: hear, Lord, the prayer of your servant, and receive with your ears the petition of your thing formed;
revisions.to: Hear, Lord, the prayer of your servant, and give ear to the petition of your creature;
revisions.rationale: First, 'hear' should be capitalized as the start of the sentence. Second, 'auribus percipe' is a standard liturgical idiom (often translating Greek ἐνωτίζεσθαι / Hebrew הַאֲזִינָה) that is better rendered as 'give ear to' rather than the overly literal 'receive with your ears.' Finally, Latin 'figmentum' (translating Greek πλάσμα) refers to humanity as formed from dust; 'creature' or 'handiwork' conveys this theological humility naturally, whereas 'thing formed' is clunky.

revisions.from: having suffered through jealousy many outrages and tortures
revisions.to: having suffered many outrages and tortures because of jealousy
revisions.rationale: The current word order is slightly clunky. Translating 'διὰ ζῆλος' as 'because of jealousy' and moving it after the objects of their suffering improves the English flow while maintaining exact correspondence to the Greek.

revisions.from: brought down into a furnace of fire
revisions.to: shut up in a furnace of fire
revisions.rationale: The Greek verb is κατείργω (aorist passive κατείρχθησαν), which means 'to shut in, confine, imprison' (BDAG/LSJ), not 'to bring down' (which would be κατάγω). The youths were confined or shut up in the furnace.

revisions.from: by the outlawed
revisions.to: by transgressors
revisions.rationale: The Greek word παρανόμων (from παράνομος) means 'transgressing the law' or 'lawbreaking'. 'Outlawed' incorrectly implies that these individuals had been stripped of legal protection or banished by the state, whereas the author's point is their active, moral violation of the law.

revisions.from: The basin
revisions.to: The mass
revisions.rationale: The Greek word χύτος refers to a 'mass' or 'volume' (of water), not a 'basin' (a container). The participle συσταθὲν ('gathered together') applies to the water itself being gathered into collections/reservoirs, which makes no physical sense if applied to a 'basin'.

revisions.from: out of a great tribe
revisions.to: out of a great forest
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'tribu' ('tribe') reflects a well-known corruption in the lost Greek transmission, where ὕλη ('forest') was misread or miscopied as φυλή ('tribe'). The parallel structure ('a grape out of a cluster' and 'a planting out of a great forest') demands 'forest,' and the daughter versions (Syriac, Ethiopic, Arabic, Armenian) all preserve 'forest.' To reflect the author's intent, the English should read 'forest'.

revisions.from: will be left from the dangers foretold
revisions.to: will survive the dangers foretold
revisions.rationale: The passive 'relinquetur' (to be left behind, remain) in this context carries the sense of surviving or being spared. 'Will be left from' is awkward English; 'will survive' or 'will be spared from' better captures the meaning of emerging safely from the predicted perils while maintaining natural English syntax.

revisions.from: the God of your servant heard me
revisions.to: God heard me, your servant,
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'exaudivit me Deus ancillae tuae' reflects a Greek Vorlage where the verb of hearing took a genitive object (εἰσήκουσέν μου... τῆς δούλης σου). The Latin translator rendered the pronoun in the accusative ('me') but left the appositional phrase in the genitive ('ancillae tuae'). The draft's 'the God of your servant' misconstrues this translationese; the intended meaning is 'God heard me, your servant.'

revisions.from: and while a place of repentance was still open to them did not understand but scorned it—for these it is necessary to know after death in torment.
revisions.to: and while a place of repentance was still open to them, they did not understand but scorned it—these must know it after death in torment.
revisions.rationale: The English draft lacks a subject for 'did not understand' (`intellexerunt`). Additionally, `hos oportet... cognoscere` uses the accusative `hos` as the subject of the infinitive ('these must know'), not as a dative ('for these it is necessary'). Finally, `cognoscere` requires an implied object in English ('know it') to complete the thought and connect back to the rejection described in the previous verses.

revisions.from: Do not attend to
revisions.to: Do not pay attention to
revisions.rationale: In modern English, 'attend to' often carries the meaning of 'deal with' or 'take care of' (e.g., 'attend to a problem'). The Latin `adtendas` (from `attendo`) here means 'to direct the mind toward,' 'to pay attention to,' or 'to consider,' functioning in parallel with `aspicias` ('look upon') in v. 25 and `cogites` ('think on') in v. 27. 'Pay attention to' or 'consider' avoids the modern ambiguity.

revisions.from: and the Master himself of all things also spoke
revisions.to: and the Master of all things himself also spoke
revisions.rationale: In English, placing the reflexive pronoun 'himself' before the genitive modifier 'of all things' awkwardly splits the noun phrase. It should follow the complete title 'the Master of all things' (ὁ δεσπότης τῶν ἁπάντων).

revisions.from: adding also a good intention:
revisions.to: adding also a good pronouncement:
revisions.rationale: While γνώμη (gnōmē) can mean 'intention' or 'purpose', here it introduces the spoken quotation in 8:3. In this context, it carries the sense of a 'maxim', 'sentence', or 'pronouncement' (BDAG γνώμη 2). Translating it as 'intention' obscures the fact that God is adding a specific spoken declaration.

revisions.from: the matters being sought from us among you
revisions.to: the matters disputed among you
revisions.rationale: The participle ἐπιζητουμένων (from ἐπιζητέω) in this context means 'disputed' or 'debated'. The draft inserts 'from us', which is absent from the Greek and distorts the historical meaning; the matters are being disputed 'among you' (παρ' ὑμῖν), not necessarily sought from Rome.

revisions.from: and, being within the rule of submission, to manage
revisions.to: and you taught them, being within the rule of submission, to manage
revisions.rationale: The draft completely omits the main verb ἐδιδάσκετε ('you taught' / 'you were teaching'), incorrectly making the final clause dependent on the earlier verb παρηγγέλλετε ('you were instructing'). This breaks the author's rhetorical sequence of imperfect verbs applied to the different groups.

revisions.from: to think thoughts marked by moderation and dignity
revisions.to: to think moderate and dignified thoughts
revisions.rationale: The Greek 'μέτρια καὶ σεμνὰ νοεῖν' (assuming the source's 'μέτρα' is an OCR error for the standard 'μέτρια') is straightforward. The draft's phrasing is unnecessarily wordy and dilutes the directness of the instruction.

revisions.from: if you will see
revisions.to: if you see
revisions.rationale: While 'ὄψῃ' is future indicative (or aorist subjunctive), English idiom for a simple conditional clause ('εἴ ... ὄψῃ') naturally uses the present tense ('if you see') rather than the awkward 'if you will see'.

revisions.from: if he does not trust even his own servants, and has perceived something crooked even in his angels?
revisions.to: if he does not trust his servants, and has perceived something crooked in his angels?
revisions.rationale: The subject of πιστεύει and ἐπενόησεν is the Lord (quoting Job 4:18). By adding 'even his own', the draft strongly implies the subject is the mortal man (ἀνήρ) distrusting his human household servants, which destroys the author's a fortiori argument about God finding fault with heavenly beings. Removing the added words allows 'his angels' to naturally clarify that the subject has shifted back to the Lord.

revisions.from: from which we ourselves also are from the same clay.
revisions.to: of which same clay we ourselves are also made.
revisions.rationale: The Greek relative clause `ἐξ ὧν... ἐκ τοῦ αὐτοῦ πηλοῦ` is a slightly redundant LXX construction. The draft's literal rendering creates ungrammatical English redundancy. Condensing it to 'of which same clay we ourselves are also made' preserves the author's exact meaning naturally.

revisions.from: For what things you prepared for them
revisions.to: For what they prepared
revisions.rationale: The provided source text contains an OCR error (`ἐκείνοις ἡτοίμασας` instead of `ἐκεῖνοι ἡτοίμασαν`). The author is quoting Job 5:5 (LXX), which refers to the wealth that the fools have prepared/gathered. Translating the OCR error as 'what things you prepared for them' breaks the sense of the quotation. It should be corrected to reflect the actual critical text ('what they prepared').

revisions.from: from the will of God
revisions.to: by the will of God
revisions.rationale: While 'ἐκ' literally denotes source or origin ('from'), when paired with 'θελήματος' (will), the natural English idiom that carries the exact same force is 'by the will of God'. 'From the will' sounds slightly wooden and distracts from the rhetorical flow.

revisions.from: and he will make glad those who remain for four hundred years.
revisions.to: and for four hundred years he will make glad those who remain.
revisions.rationale: In English, "those who remain for four hundred years" implies they have been waiting or remaining for 400 years. The Latin `annis quadringentis` (ablative of duration) modifies the main verb, indicating the duration of the Messiah's reign and their rejoicing. Moving the phrase clarifies the author's eschatological timeline.

revisions.from: they themselves will pass over into torments
revisions.to: some of them will pass over into torment
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'de eis' is partitive ('some of them' or 'from among them'), likely reflecting a Greek ἐξ αὐτῶν / Hebrew מֵהֶם. It does not mean 'they themselves' (which would require 'ipsi'). Furthermore, 'cruciamentum' is singular ('torment'), consistent with its use in verse 84, rather than plural.

revisions.from: not to give the earth to Adam, or else, once it had been given
revisions.to: for the earth not to have produced Adam, or else, when it had already produced him
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'non dare terram Adam' is an accusative-and-infinitive construction where 'terram' is the subject and 'Adam' is the object. In the idiom of 4 Ezra (cf. 3:5, where the dust 'dedit Adam'), 'dare' means 'to produce' or 'yield.' The draft mistranslates this as God giving the earth to Adam, which misses the author's recurring theological motif of the earth bringing forth humanity and distorts the active verb 'dedisset' (when the earth had produced him).

revisions.from: someone who wishes to enter should want to see the sea or master it, how will he come into the breadth
revisions.to: anyone earnestly desires to enter the sea to see it or to master it, how will he be able to come into the broad place
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'volens voluerit' is a calque of a Semitic infinitive absolute construction (via Greek) indicating intensity; it should be rendered 'earnestly desires' or 'truly wishes' rather than the awkward 'who wishes... should want'. The draft also incorrectly splits 'ingredi mare' ('to enter the sea'), leaving 'enter' without an object, and omits 'poterit' ('be able').

revisions.from: For all who have been born are mixed with iniquities, and are full of sins and loaded with transgressions.
revisions.to: What does it profit that we will surely be saved, if we will be severely tormented? For all who have been born are entangled in iniquities, and are full of sins and burdened with transgressions.
revisions.rationale: The draft completely omits the first half of the Latin source text assigned to this verse ('quid prodest...'). Additionally, 'salutati saluabimur' (where 'salutati' is a common scribal variant for 'saluati') and 'tormento tormentabimur' are Latin calques for the Hebrew infinitive absolute, conveying certainty and intensity ('surely be saved', 'severely tormented'). Finally, 'entangled in' and 'burdened with' better capture the moral weight and apocalyptic register of 'conmixti' and 'grauidi' than the awkward 'mixed with' and colloquial 'loaded'.

revisions.from: The seventh way, greater than all the ways mentioned above: they waste away in confusion and are consumed in shame and wither in fears, because they see the glory of the Most High, before whom they sinned while they lived, and before whom they are to be judged in the last times.
revisions.to: The seventh way is greater than all the ways mentioned above, because they will waste away in confusion and be consumed in horrors and wither in fears, seeing the glory of the Most High, before whom they sinned while they lived, and before whom they will begin to be judged in the last times.
revisions.rationale: There are several compounding issues here: 1) 'horroribus' means 'horrors' or 'terrors', not 'shame'. 2) The verbs 'detabescent', 'consumentur', and 'marcescent' are explicitly future tense, not present. 3) 'quoniam' (because) introduces the reason the seventh way is greatest ('because they will waste away'), whereas the draft moves 'because' to translate the participle 'uidentes' (seeing), breaking the causal logic. 4) 'incipient' (will begin) is omitted from the final clause ('incipient... iudicari').

revisions.from: from these things
revisions.to: from God
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'ab his' ('from these') is a recognized translation error, reflecting a misreading of the lost Greek ἀπ' αὐτοῦ ('from him') as ἀπ' αὐτῶν ('from them'). The Syriac, Ethiopic, Arabic, and Armenian daughter versions all preserve 'from him' (i.e., God). Given the theological weight of the 'evil heart' (cor malum / yetzer hara) in 4 Ezra, the author's intent was that the evil heart alienated humanity from God, not 'from these things'.

revisions.from: their food became enough to satisfy me.
revisions.to: the food from them was enough to satisfy me.
revisions.rationale: In English, 'their food' naturally implies possession (the food the herbs themselves consume), whereas the Latin `esca earum` (genitive of source or material) refers to the food derived from the herbs. Rendering it as 'the food from them' removes this slight ambiguity while accurately reflecting the Latin.

revisions.from: knowing what was to come with confidence
revisions.to: knowing with confidence what was to come
revisions.rationale: The current English word order creates a slight ambiguity, making it sound as though the future events possessed confidence. The Greek `μετὰ πεποιθήσεως` precedes `γινώσκων`, modifying the act of knowing. Moving 'with confidence' before 'what was to come' resolves the ambiguity and better reflects the Greek phrasing.

revisions.from: asked from the elders
revisions.to: asked the elders
revisions.rationale: While 'asked from' literally renders ᾐτήσατο παρὰ, 'asked the elders' or 'requested of the elders' is a more natural English idiom that carries the exact same force and meaning.

revisions.from: taking our place under obedience, should reach the goal
revisions.to: fulfilling the place of obedience, we should be quiet, so that having ceased from the vain sedition, we may reach the goal
revisions.rationale: The English draft omits a major clause present in the critical text (Lightfoot 1889): 'ἡσυχάσαι, ὅπως κοπάσαντες τῆς ματαίας στάσεως' (to be quiet, so that having ceased from the vain sedition). The provided Greek source text contains a severe OCR haplography ('ὑπακατὰ τὸν' instead of 'ὑπακοῆς τόπον ἀναπληρώσαντας ἡσυχάσαι, ὅπως κοπάσαντες τῆς ματαίας στάσεως ἐπὶ τὸν'), causing the draft to miss the author's crucial appeal to cease from 'sedition' (στάσις), which is the central theme of the letter.

revisions.from: “Blessed is one born of woman, short-lived. Do not become excessive in words.”
revisions.to: “Blessed is the short-lived one born of woman. Do not be a person of many words.”
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'one born of woman, short-lived' is awkward in English; 'the short-lived one born of woman' better captures the Greek substantive phrase `γεννητὸς γυναικὸς ὀλιγόβιος`. Additionally, `μὴ πολὺς ἐν ῥήμασιν γίνου` is an idiom meaning 'do not be a person of many words' or 'do not be abundant in words,' rather than 'do not become excessive.' The imperative `γίνου` here functions more like 'be' than 'become'.

revisions.from: remove your wicked deeds from before my eyes
revisions.to: remove your wicked deeds from your souls before my eyes
revisions.rationale: The draft completely omits the phrase 'ἀπὸ τῶν ψυχῶν ὑμῶν' ('from your souls'), which is present in the Greek text (and in the LXX of Isaiah 1:16 that Clement is quoting).

revisions.from: And he answered me and said, “I will show you this also; but do not mix yourself with those who have shown contempt, and do not count yourself among those who are tormented.
revisions.to: those times come in which you will begin to renew the creation, or are we tormented at once?” And he answered me and said, “I will show you this also; but as for you, do not mix yourself with them
revisions.rationale: The English draft is misaligned with the Latin verse boundaries. It omits the first half of the Latin source for 7:76 ('ueniant tempora illa...') and incorrectly includes text from 7:77 ('qui speruerunt...'). To accurately reflect the specific Latin span of 7:76, the English must be realigned. Furthermore, the Latin 'tu autem' contains an emphatic pronoun ('but as for you') which the draft missed.

revisions.from: are we kept
revisions.to: will we surely be kept
revisions.rationale: The Latin 'conseruati conseruabimur' reflects a Semitic infinitive absolute construction (participle + finite verb) denoting emphasis ('surely be kept'). Furthermore, 'conseruabimur' is future tense, which the English present 'are we kept' loses.

revisions.from: Therefore there will be no grief over their destruction, just as there will be joy over those to whom salvation has been persuaded.”
revisions.to: For there should be no grief over their destruction, just as there will be joy over those to whom salvation is assured.”
revisions.rationale: Latin 'quoniam' means 'for' or 'because', not 'therefore' (which would be 'ergo' or 'ideo'). Additionally, 'persuasa est' with a dative ('quibus') means 'is assured' or 'made certain'; 'salvation has been persuaded' is unidiomatic in English and misrepresents the semantic force of the Latin. Finally, 'esset' is imperfect subjunctive, better rendered 'should be' or 'would be' rather than the indicative 'will be'.

revisions.from: those who dwell in silence in it
revisions.to: those who dwell in it in silence
revisions.rationale: The Latin `qui in eo silentio habitant` parallels `qui in eam dormiunt` (those who sleep in it). Shifting the prepositional phrase 'in it' to immediately follow the verb makes the English flow more naturally and better reflects the parallel structure of the Latin clauses.

revisions.from: with the elders who have been appointed
revisions.to: with the appointed elders
revisions.rationale: Translating the attributive participle phrase τῶν καθεσταμένων πρεσβυτέρων as 'the appointed elders' is more concise and natural in English while retaining the exact meaning.

revisions.from: let our whole body be held together
revisions.to: let our whole body be preserved
revisions.rationale: The source text contains an OCR error ('Συνξέσϑω' for 'Σωζέσθω' / 'Σῳζέσθω'). The author wrote 'σωζέσθω' (let it be saved/preserved). The draft's 'be held together' attempts to translate the corrupted text or guesses at 'συνεχέσθω', but the manuscript tradition reads 'σωζέσθω'.

revisions.from: according as he was appointed in his own gift
revisions.to: just as he was appointed in his gift
revisions.rationale: 'According as' is archaic and slightly awkward. 'Just as he was appointed in his gift' reads more naturally while preserving the force of 'καθὼς ἐτέθη'. Furthermore, 'αὐτοῦ' is simply 'his', not 'his own' (which would typically be ἑαυτοῦ or ἰδίῳ).

revisions.from: but let him allow himself to be borne witness to by another
revisions.to: but let him allow another to bear witness to him
revisions.rationale: The literal rendering of the passive infinitive (`ἑαυτὸν μαρτυρεῖσθαι`) results in the clunky English phrase 'allow himself to be borne witness to'. Converting this to an active construction in English preserves the exact meaning and the lexical connection to the preceding 'bear witness to himself' (`ἑαυτῷ μαρτυρείτω`) while reading much more naturally.

revisions.from: supplies him self-control
revisions.to: supplies him with self-control
revisions.rationale: In English, the verb 'supply' typically takes the preposition 'with' when the recipient is the object (e.g., 'supplies him with self-control'), or uses 'to' if the item is the object ('supplies self-control to him'). 'Supplies him self-control' feels slightly colloquial.

revisions.from: suppliants of his mercy and kindness
revisions.to: suppliants for his mercy and kindness
revisions.rationale: The Greek genitives τοῦ ἐλέους καὶ τῆς χρηστότητος function objectively here (indicating what is being sought). In English, one is a suppliant 'for' something, not 'of' it.

revisions.from: in harmony, two by two
revisions.to: in harmony
revisions.rationale: The Greek text does not contain 'two by two' (which would be 'δύο δύο' or similar). The phrase is simply 'ἐν ὁμονοίᾳ' (in concord/harmony; note the OCR error 'ὁμοίᾳ' in the provided source text). Adding 'two by two' imports details from Genesis that Clement did not include here.

revisions.from: The vile, and those who set themselves against his will.
revisions.to: The wicked and those who set themselves against his will.
revisions.rationale: In moral contexts, 'φαῦλος' typically denotes the 'wicked' or 'bad' rather than the more emotionally charged 'vile'. Additionally, removing the comma better reflects the single Greek article (οἱ) governing both descriptors, binding them together as a single group of enemies.

revisions.from: But concerning his Son it says thus:
revisions.to: But concerning his Son, the Master spoke thus:
revisions.rationale: The draft completely omits the phrase `εἶπεν ὁ δεσπότης` ("the Master said"). The term `δεσπότης` is a crucial and frequent title for God in 1 Clement (as seen just above in 36:2), and its omission loses the author's explicit attribution of the Psalm.

revisions.from: the city now hidden will appear
revisions.to: the appearing city
revisions.rationale: The Latin source is 'apparescens civitas' (the appearing city). The draft translates this as 'the city now hidden will appear', importing the scholarly reconstruction of the lost Greek (ἡ νῦν μὴ φαινομένη) against the explicit Latin text. The Latin translator misread the Greek as ἡ νύμφη φαινομένη (the appearing bride), which is why the Latin has 'sponsa et apparescens civitas'. The translation must reflect the Latin source provided.

revisions.from: relieved from their iniquities
revisions.to: relieved of their iniquities
revisions.rationale: In English idiom, one is 'relieved of' a burden (such as iniquities or guilt) rather than 'relieved from' it. This better captures the unburdening sense of the Latin 'adleventur... de'.
